title: Giant nonlinear optical chirality in twisted heterobilayers
abstract: Twisting two dissimilar monolayer semiconductors induces structural chirality that remains largely elusive in linear optics but becomes remarkably pronounced in the nonlinear regime. Here we demonstrate that MoS2/WSe2 heterobilayers exhibit giant, twist-tunable nonlinear chirality in second-harmonic generation (SHG). The sign of SHG circular dichroism is governed by structural handedness, and its magnitude reaches 1.96 near a 30° twist angle under 1260-nm excitation, approaching the theoretical limit of 2. Furthermore, reversed chirality is observed when light is incident from opposite directions. Using a layer-resolved model, we attribute this phenomenon to helicity-dependent interference between the two monolayer SHG fields, mediated by a nonlinear Pancharatnam-Berry phase. These findings establish that the relative orientation of atomically thin layers can deterministically control nonlinear chiral responses, identifying twisted 2D heterostructures as a versatile platform for nonlinear chiral photonics, frequency conversion, and ultracompact light-matter interfaces.
one_liner: Twisted MoS2/WSe2 bilayers show near-limit, twist-controlled SHG circular dichroism for nonlinear chiral photonics.
why_it_matters: It turns atomic-layer twist angle into a deterministic control knob for ultrathin nonlinear chirality and frequency-conversion interfaces.
detailed_summary: The work demonstrates giant nonlinear optical chirality in MoS2/WSe2 heterobilayers through second-harmonic generation. SHG circular dichroism changes sign with structural handedness and reaches 1.96 near a 30 degree twist under 1260 nm excitation, close to the theoretical maximum. A layer-resolved model attributes the effect to helicity-dependent interference of monolayer SHG fields mediated by a nonlinear Pancharatnam-Berry phase.
main_result: The authors demonstrate twist-tunable SHG circular dichroism up to 1.96 with handedness and propagation-direction dependent reversal.
application_angle: The platform is relevant for ultracompact nonlinear chiral photonics, frequency conversion, and engineered light-matter interfaces.

title: Coherence-Enhanced Quantum Battery Charging with Ergotropy Stabilization
abstract: Quantum batteries utilize nonclassical resources to achieve charging speed and energy storage performances that surpass classical thermodynamic limits. However, the practical realization of quantum batteries is often constrained by the inevitable environment-induced dissipation of both stored ergotropy and coherence. To actively counteract these losses, we propose a dual-channel coherence framework that exploits dark-state protection to stabilize ergotropy. We conduct, for the first time, an investigation of the synergistic interplay between internal charger coherence and reservoir squeezing, the latter acting as a source of external coherence. In the resource-efficient regime where charger and battery sizes are comparable, our study shows that internal charger coherence and reservoir squeezing jointly enhance the transient charging power. Crucially, initial charger coherence is the fundamental resource for maximizing and stabilizing steady-state ergotropy through dark-state protection. Our analysis reveals that these advantages are driven by the buildup of local battery coherence, which emerges from the integration of both internal and external coherence sources. These results offer a robust pathway for high-power, stabilized energy storage in quantum architectures.
one_liner: A dual-coherence quantum battery model uses charger coherence and squeezed reservoirs to stabilize steady-state ergotropy.
why_it_matters: It identifies coherence-protected dark states as a route to maintaining usable stored energy despite dissipation in quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a quantum battery charging framework combining internal charger coherence with external coherence from a squeezed reservoir. In resource-efficient charger-battery size regimes, the two coherence channels enhance transient charging power, while initial charger coherence is essential for maximizing and stabilizing steady-state ergotropy. The mechanism is linked to local battery coherence buildup and dark-state protection against dissipative loss.
main_result: The study shows that internal charger coherence plus reservoir squeezing can boost charging power and stabilize ergotropy through dark-state protection.
application_angle: The result informs design principles for dissipative quantum devices that need robust, usable energy storage or controlled work extraction.

title: Crosstalk-free Chiral Anomaly Bulk States in Photonic Crystals
abstract: Ultracompact cladding-free waveguide arrays with zero inter-channel spacing and negligible crosstalk open a new avenue for high-density integrated photonic circuits. However, existing cladding-free waveguide arrays typically rely on conventional trivial bulk modes, making them highly susceptible to scattering losses at sharp bends or in the presence of obstacles and defects. To overcome this limitation, we theoretically propose and experimentally demonstrate a robust, crosstalk-free, and cladding-free photonic waveguide array based on chiral anomaly bulk states (CABSs) in photonic crystals. By interfacing distinct Dirac photonic crystals that host Dirac cones at different high-symmetry points (Γ and K) in the Brillouin zone and carefully engineering the boundary conditions, the boundary-induced CABSs in adjacent channels become effectively decoupled due to a large momentum separation, thereby eliminating inter-channel crosstalk. More importantly, we experimentally demonstrate that these crosstalk-free CABSs are robust to perturbations, including metallic obstacles, air defects, and sharp bends. We further extend the CABS-based waveguide array to two dimensions and demonstrate a cladding-free triangular resonator and a crosstalk-free waveguide crossing, both of which are previously unattainable. Our work establishes a new design paradigm for cladding-free, crosstalk-free, and ultracompact topological photonic devices, paving the way for robust, highly integrated photonic circuits.
one_liner: Photonic-crystal chiral anomaly bulk states enable cladding-free waveguide arrays with negligible crosstalk and bend robustness.
why_it_matters: It offers a topological route to denser integrated photonics without the spacing penalties and fragility of conventional waveguide arrays.
detailed_summary: The authors theoretically propose and experimentally demonstrate cladding-free photonic waveguide arrays based on chiral anomaly bulk states. Interfaces between Dirac photonic crystals with cones at different Brillouin-zone points create adjacent channels that are decoupled by large momentum separation. Experiments show robust transport through metallic obstacles, air defects, and sharp bends, and the concept is extended to 2D devices including a triangular resonator and waveguide crossing.
main_result: The work demonstrates robust, crosstalk-free, zero-spacing photonic channels and associated compact topological devices.
application_angle: This can support high-density integrated photonic circuits where routing density, defect tolerance, and low crosstalk are critical.

title: Non-Stationary Decoherence in Superconducting Qubits: Memory Multi-Fractional Brownian Motion and a Time-Dependent Quantum Brownian Motion Extension
abstract: Building upon our prior work [1], we present a unified stochastic drift model (SdM) for superconducting charge qubits based on memory multi-fractional Brownian motion (mmFBM). The classical sector employs a time-dependent Hurst exponent H(t) and adaptive memory kernel K(t,s), capturing non-stationary 1/f^beta noise and long-range temporal correlations inaccessible to conventional models. The quantum extension is formulated via a time-dependent Caldeira--Leggett environment with spectral density J(omega;t) = eta(t) omega_c^{1-s(t)} omega^{s(t)} exp(-omega/omega_c), where s(t) = 2H(t)-1, consistently reproducing beta(t) = 2H(t)-1. Four central results emerge: (1) relaxation and noise amplitudes act independently on energy decay; (2) time-varying H(t) matches experimental 1/f spectra more accurately than any constant exponent; (3) adaptive kernel dynamics preserve correlations without artificial damping; and (4) simulations predict coherence times (T1 ~ 5.00 x 10^6 ns, T2 ~ 4.18 x 10^5 ns) consistent with theory when charge noise dominates. The qubit exhibits stretched-exponential Ramsey and echo decay, non-Markovian dephasing, and a temperature-driven quantum-to-classical crossover. We derive the effective time-local Lindblad master equation, establish the classical mmFBM limit at high temperatures, and provide experimentally testable scaling relations. The non-exponential decay patterns reveal fundamental limitations of Markovian approaches, and the framework guides the design of noise-resilient qubit architectures.
one_liner: A nonstationary stochastic noise model captures memory-rich 1/f decoherence in superconducting charge qubits.
why_it_matters: Better modeling of time-varying noise and non-Markovian decay can guide calibration, diagnosis, and noise-resilient superconducting qubit design.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a stochastic drift model for superconducting charge qubits using memory multi-fractional Brownian motion with time-dependent Hurst exponent and adaptive memory kernel. Its quantum extension uses a time-dependent Caldeira-Leggett bath with spectral exponent tied to the evolving Hurst parameter. The framework predicts stretched-exponential Ramsey and echo decay, non-Markovian dephasing, a temperature-driven quantum-to-classical crossover, and experimentally testable scaling laws.
main_result: The authors present a time-dependent classical and quantum noise model that reproduces nonstationary 1/f spectra and non-exponential qubit decoherence.
application_angle: It is practically relevant for qubit noise spectroscopy, decoherence forecasting, and engineering control strategies against long-memory charge noise.

title: A Wafer-Scale Heterogeneous III-V-on-Silicon Nitride Quantum Photonic Platform
abstract: Heterogeneous integration of gain and strongly nonlinear materials with ultra-low-loss silicon nitride (SiN) photonics offers a route to scalable quantum circuits, but concurrent wafer-scale manufacturability, low interlayer loss, and high performance have been challenging to realize. Here we demonstrate a wafer-scale III-V-on-SiN quantum photonic platform that directly integrates III-V layers to foundry-fabricated SiN circuits. The SiN layer provides 200-300 nm thick waveguides with $<1$ dB/m loss and a mature passive photonics ecosystem, while III-V materials provide large $χ^{\left(2\right)}$ and $χ^{\left(3\right)}$ nonlinearities for parametric gain, frequency conversion and quantum light generation. Adiabatic interlayer couplers yield $<25$ mdB loss to InGaP waveguides and resonators with intrinsic quality factors exceeding $10^6$, enabling $15\times$ brighter entanglement sources and efficient nonlinear conversion on SiN. Integrated components--including low-loss beam splitters, waveguide crossers, and tunable interferometers--are complemented by III-V lasers and InP photodetectors with amplifiers achieving up to $99^{+1}_{-12}\%$ quantum efficiency and $3$ GHz bandwidth. This architecture unites ultra-efficient sources, nonlinear elements and detectors on a wafer-scale, low-loss platform, establishing a path toward large-scale, low-noise quantum photonic systems.
one_liner: A wafer-scale III-V-on-SiN platform integrates low-loss passives, nonlinear sources, lasers, and detectors for quantum photonics.
why_it_matters: It addresses manufacturable integration of sources, nonlinear functions, and detection on a low-loss photonic platform for scalable quantum circuits.
detailed_summary: The work demonstrates heterogeneous integration of III-V materials with foundry-fabricated, ultra-low-loss silicon nitride photonics. Adiabatic interlayer couplers show below 25 mdB loss into InGaP, resonators exceed intrinsic Q of 10^6, and the platform enables 15x brighter entanglement sources. The integrated stack also includes passive SiN components, III-V lasers, and InP photodetectors with amplifier-assisted quantum efficiency up to 99% and 3 GHz bandwidth.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a manufacturable III-V-on-SiN quantum photonic platform combining low-loss routing, nonlinear generation, active gain, and efficient detection.
application_angle: The platform is directly relevant to large-scale quantum photonic processors, low-noise sources, frequency conversion, and quantum communication hardware.

title: Spin-force from a Nitrogen-Vacancy ensemble drives a 100 mg levitated resonator
abstract: The force experienced by a spin in a magnetic field gradient underlies many proposals for hybrid quantum systems. These include schemes for mechanically mediated quantum gates, spin squeezing, searches for exotic forces, and motional superpositions for probing the interface between quantum and gravity. Yet, experimentally observing this spin-force for anything larger than atomic scales has proved challenging. In our work, we demonstrate controllable Center-of-Mass motion of a $128 \rm\: mg$ diamagnetically levitated oscillator due to force from an ensemble of Nitrogen-Vacancy (NV) defects in diamond. We induce coherent motion in the oscillator by periodic optical initialisation of the NV spin states, achieving motional amplitudes exceeding $100 \rm\:nm$. Our results mark a key milestone towards spin-based engineering of motional states deep in the high-mass regime.
one_liner: An NV ensemble exerts a controllable spin-force that drives coherent motion of a 128 mg levitated mechanical resonator.
why_it_matters: It pushes spin-mechanical control into a high-mass regime relevant to force sensing, hybrid quantum systems, and macroscopic motion engineering.
detailed_summary: The authors demonstrate center-of-mass actuation of a 128 mg diamagnetically levitated oscillator using force from an ensemble of nitrogen-vacancy defects in diamond. Periodic optical initialization of NV spin states generates coherent oscillator motion with amplitudes exceeding 100 nm. The result experimentally connects spin-dependent forces to controllable mechanical motion far above atomic mass scales.
main_result: The experiment drives coherent motion of a 128 mg levitated oscillator via controllable spin-force from NV defects.
application_angle: This advances spin-mechanical interfaces for precision force sensing, mechanically mediated quantum control, and tests of macroscopic quantum motion.

title: Optical Neural Networks from Coherent Transient Dynamics in Waveguide QED
abstract: Optical neural networks promise ultrafast, low-energy information processing by performing computation directly with photons. Current implementations, however, are largely restricted to steady-state operation and rely on high-latency electro-optical conversion for nonlinear activation. To address these limitations, we propose an all-optical fully connected neural network architecture in which the basic neuronal functions are realized by coherent transient quantum dynamics. Within this framework, phase-tunable nonlocal interference in a giant cavity implements programmable synaptic weights; an integrator operating in the bad cavity regime performs temporal summation by coherently combining sequential wavepackets; and transient Rabi dynamics of a driven two-level system provide nonlinear activation. Full-physics simulations demonstrate high classification accuracy on MNIST and colored-object recognition tasks. These results eliminate the optoelectronic activation bottleneck, reduce latency, and establish transient light-matter dynamics as a native physical resource for high-dimensional nonlinear information processing, paving the way toward fully optical neuromorphic computing.
one_liner: Proposes an all-optical neural network using transient waveguide-QED dynamics for weights, integration, and nonlinear activation.
why_it_matters: It targets the electro-optic activation bottleneck in optical neural networks with native coherent light-matter nonlinearities.
detailed_summary: The work designs a fully connected optical neural network whose neuron primitives are implemented through coherent transient dynamics rather than steady-state photonic circuits. Phase-tunable nonlocal interference in a giant cavity supplies programmable weights, bad-cavity dynamics integrate sequential wavepackets, and driven two-level-system Rabi dynamics provide nonlinear activation. Full-physics simulations report strong performance on MNIST and colored-object classification tasks.
main_result: Full-physics simulations show high-accuracy optical classification with all-optical weighting, integration, and nonlinear activation.
application_angle: The architecture is relevant to low-latency neuromorphic photonic processors that avoid repeated optical-electrical-optical conversion.

title: InterQ: Communication-Aware Scheduling Across Modular QPUs with Classical and Quantum Links
abstract: As quantum computing scales toward practical workloads, future systems are expected to move beyond single monolithic processors toward modular architectures that connect multiple QPUs. Different platforms realize this modularity through different communication models: superconducting systems rely on real-time classical links and dynamic-circuit coordination, trapped-ion systems use photonic interconnects for remote entanglement, and neutral-atom systems provide strong intra-core connectivity with proposed optical links for inter-core communication. This heterogeneity makes communication-aware scheduling essential for shared modular quantum cloud environments. We present InterQ, a communication-aware scheduler for modular QPU architectures with heterogeneous communication models. InterQ jointly considers qubit capacity, placement, parallel execution, and communication-driven dependencies across distributed subcircuits, while enabling adaptive circuit cutting to reduce makespan while balancing fidelity and communication overhead. The framework distinguishes classical-link execution, where measurement and feedforward impose synchronization constraints, from quantum-link execution, where entanglement distribution and state transfer determine coordination cost. Using a unified simulation framework to compare superconducting, trapped-ion, and neutral-atom modular systems, InterQ shows how communication models and scheduler-driven cutting decisions affect throughput, latency, and fidelity. Across evaluated workloads, InterQ exposes an architecture-dependent tradeoff: neutral-atom modular QPUs achieve the highest fidelity, superconducting systems minimize runtime, and trapped-ion systems provide a balanced intermediate profile across fidelity and makespan.
one_liner: Introduces InterQ, a scheduler for modular quantum processors with heterogeneous classical and quantum communication links.
why_it_matters: Communication-aware scheduling will be central as cloud quantum systems move from single QPUs to linked superconducting, ion, and neutral-atom modules.
detailed_summary: InterQ jointly optimizes qubit placement, subcircuit parallelism, communication dependencies, and adaptive circuit cutting across modular QPU systems. The framework separates classical-link execution, dominated by measurement/feedforward synchronization, from quantum-link execution, dominated by entanglement distribution or state transfer. Simulations compare superconducting, trapped-ion, and neutral-atom modular architectures and quantify tradeoffs among runtime, throughput, and fidelity.
main_result: InterQ exposes architecture-dependent scheduling tradeoffs: superconducting systems minimize runtime, neutral atoms maximize fidelity, and trapped ions sit between them.
application_angle: The tool is directly relevant to resource management and workload scheduling in future distributed quantum cloud hardware.

title: Generation of deterministic multi-mode intensity squeezing in a train of ultra-short pulses by unbalanced SU(1,1) interferometers
abstract: The continuous variable quantum state generated by time-domain multiplexed optical parametric amplifier (OPA) is attractive because of the potential of enlarging the mode scale. Currently, the duration of temporal mode is longer than 100 ns since the OPA is pumped by the continuous wave laser, which restricts the scale of quantum state. Here we demonstrate multi-mode intensity squeezing localized in a train of short pulses with duration of $\sim10$ ps by using an unbalanced SU(1,1) interferometer (USUI), where the mode-locked laser is exploited as the pump and the time-domain multiplexing is realized by the combination of optical delay and nonlinear beam splitter. Using the pulse resolved joint measurements, we reveal the correlation structure of the state is unique and fundamentally different from previous approaches. Due to the globe quantum correlation, the intensity squeezing not only depends on the gain of OPAs but also ties to the mode number $M$ of joint measurement. We experimentally perform joint measurement among 30 modes and show the intensity noise lower than shot noise level by $\sim0.9$ dB is achievable for $M>10$. Our investigations open the door for generating ultra-large scale quantum state by pulse pumped USUI.
one_liner: Demonstrates deterministic multimode intensity squeezing in ~10 ps pulse trains using unbalanced SU(1,1) interferometers.
why_it_matters: Picosecond time-domain multiplexing could greatly increase the scale and bandwidth of continuous-variable quantum optical states.
detailed_summary: The experiment uses a mode-locked pump, optical delay, and nonlinear beam splitting in an unbalanced SU(1,1) interferometer to generate squeezed temporal modes localized in ~10 ps pulses. Pulse-resolved joint measurements reveal a correlation structure distinct from continuous-wave OPA approaches. Joint measurements over 30 modes show intensity noise about 0.9 dB below shot noise for mode number M greater than 10.
main_result: The authors experimentally generate deterministic multimode intensity squeezing across 30 pulse modes with sub-shot-noise joint intensity for M>10.
application_angle: This provides a route toward ultralarge-scale continuous-variable photonic resources for quantum networking, computing, and metrology.

title: Amplification of Weak Forces via Parametric Interactions and Non-Markovian Effects in Cavity Optomechanics
abstract: Weak force amplification describes the process of amplifying a faint low-frequency signal by means of an additional high-frequency modulation, which plays a vital role in quantum sensing and high-precision measurement. However, the potential enhancement of weak-force amplification in non-Markovian environments has received little attention. In this paper, we firstly study the amplification of weak forces within cavity-optomechanical systems incorporating a degenerate optical parametric amplifier (DOPA) under the Markovian assumption, which can be amplified via using two high-frequency signals via vibrational resonance through adjusting the strength and phase of the DOPA with different pumping frequencies. Moreover, we extend the study of the amplification of the weak force to the non-Markovian environment composed of an ensemble of infinite oscillators. We reveal that the amplification exhibits a conversion from the non-Markovian regime to Markovian regime by controlling environmental spectral width. Such a transition facilitates a remarkable improvement in amplification, and this enhancement originates from the excitation backflow generated via the interplay between the cavity and the non-Markovian environment. By controlling DOPA to amplify weak forces, the study achieves amplification in the non-Markovian regime, offering new directions for quantum optics research.
one_liner: Studies weak-force amplification in optomechanical cavities using parametric gain and non-Markovian environmental backflow.
why_it_matters: It suggests reservoir memory and optical parametric control can enhance force readout beyond standard Markovian sensing models.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes a cavity-optomechanical force-amplification scheme incorporating a degenerate optical parametric amplifier with tunable strength, phase, and pumping frequencies. Under Markovian assumptions, high-frequency drives induce vibrational-resonance amplification of weak low-frequency forces. Extending the model to a structured oscillator bath, the authors find a tunable transition from non-Markovian to Markovian behavior, with enhanced amplification attributed to excitation backflow.
main_result: The study predicts improved weak-force amplification by combining parametric optical control with tunable non-Markovian environmental memory.
application_angle: The mechanism could inform high-precision optomechanical sensors where engineered reservoirs improve detection of faint forces.

title: Cryogenic Time-Division-Multiplexed Voltage Control for Scalable Trapped-Ion Quantum Processors
abstract: Trapped-ion quantum computers based on the quantum charge-coupled device architecture require on the order of ten trap electrodes per qubit, making the number of vacuum feedthroughs a bottleneck at the system scale. Time-division multiplexed (TDM)-based voltage control for trap electrodes provides a natural route to alleviate this constraint. However, previous studies have been limited to architectural proposals for static trap-potential compensation and room-temperature demonstrations of dynamic-electrode control, leaving cryogenic operation of TDM-based voltage control for static and dynamic electrodes experimentally unexplored. In this study, we develop and cryogenically validate TDM-based voltage control schemes for two distinct electrode classes. For static electrodes used in trap-potential compensation, we implement a 32-channel demultiplexed system operating at approximately 27~K, achieving an effective voltage update rate of 37.5~kHz with an output range of $\pm10~\mathrm{V}$ per channel. For dynamic electrodes used in ion operations, such as shuttling, we implement a four-channel demultiplexed system operating at approximately 14~K, achieving an effective voltage update rate of 1~MHz with a comparable output range. These results establish TDM-based voltage control as a practical approach for both electrode classes, providing a path for mitigating the vacuum feedthrough bottleneck in scalable trapped-ion quantum processors.
one_liner: Cryogenically validates time-division-multiplexed voltage control for both static and dynamic trapped-ion trap electrodes.
why_it_matters: Reducing electrode wiring and vacuum feedthroughs is a practical scaling bottleneck for QCCD trapped-ion processors.
detailed_summary: The study implements cryogenic TDM voltage-control electronics for two electrode classes in trapped-ion processors. A 32-channel demultiplexed static-electrode system operates near 27 K with a 37.5 kHz effective update rate and ±10 V range per channel. A four-channel dynamic-electrode system operates near 14 K with a 1 MHz effective update rate and a comparable voltage range, supporting operations such as shuttling.
main_result: The authors demonstrate cryogenic multiplexed voltage delivery for static compensation and dynamic ion-operation electrodes with practical update rates and voltage ranges.
application_angle: This is a concrete hardware path to reduce feedthrough count and wiring complexity in scalable trapped-ion quantum computers.

title: Bound state in the continuum and dynamics via phase modulation in giant-atom waveguide setups
abstract: Giant atoms, which couple to a waveguide through multiple spatially separated connection points beyond the dipole approximation, provide a versatile route for quantum information processing based on interference-induced bound states in the continuum (BICs). While multi-giant-atom architectures are being developed toward giant-atom quantum networks, the role of direct coupling between the giant atoms, in particular the associated coupling phase, in atomic dynamics remains insufficiently understood. Here we take a first step toward addressing this issue by studying a two-giant-atom waveguide-QED model. We show that the coupling phase can be used to control both the number of BICs and their profiles for both of photon and atoms. More interestingly, the presence of BICs gives rise to a variety of dynamical behaviors, providing an effective mechanism for tailoring quantum-state evolution in giant-atom waveguide-QED systems. Our results highlight coupling-phase engineering as a useful tool for controlling interference, bound states, and quantum dynamics in nonlocal light--matter interfaces.
one_liner: Shows coupling-phase control of bound states and dynamics in a two-giant-atom waveguide-QED setup.
why_it_matters: Phase engineering offers a compact control knob for nonlocal light-matter interfaces used in quantum photonic networks.
detailed_summary: The paper studies two directly coupled giant atoms interacting with a waveguide through multiple separated connection points. It shows that the phase associated with direct inter-atom coupling controls the number and spatial profiles of bound states in the continuum for both photonic and atomic components. The resulting BIC structure produces diverse dynamical regimes, enabling tailored quantum-state evolution in nonlocal waveguide-QED systems.
main_result: The authors show that coupling phase can tune BIC count, BIC profiles, and the resulting atomic-photonic dynamics.
application_angle: Such controllable interference physics is relevant for designing giant-atom quantum network nodes and waveguide-mediated quantum communication devices.

title: A System Aware Resource Allocation for Distributed Workflows in Quantum Computing Environments
abstract: Rapid advancements in cloud based platforms providing access to quantum computing capabilities have opened up several challenges for efficient usage of these highly delicate and costly devices. Although most of the current systems use a priority based access protocol, they are unable to fully support reliable, efficient, and scalable execution of larger-scale applications. To overcome this limitation, we propose a comprehensive solution for efficient allocation of quantum programs to appropriate quantum devices, considering all the relevant cost metrics into account including, fidelity, execution time and communication overhead. We also formulate use-cases for distributed quantum workflow and propose modified graph based algorithms to solve for allocation of such use-cases, assuming a hybrid classical-quantum network. Since hardware advancements in large standalone devices is an ongoing process, it is critical to investigate such distributed workflows to maximize the best utilization of current NISQ devices. Our empirical study shows that the proposed techniques perform better than state-of-the-art methods for almost all evaluation parameters, with average improvements of approximately $5\%$ in execution time, $30\%$ in communication overhead, $40\%$ in wait time and $2\%$ in fidelity, providing better solutions to efficient allocation strategies.
one_liner: Backend-aware schedulers map distributed quantum workflows to NISQ devices using fidelity, runtime, wait time, and network costs.
why_it_matters: Resource allocation is a practical bottleneck for cloud quantum workflows, especially as applications span multiple heterogeneous processors.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates distributed quantum workflow allocation across hybrid classical-quantum networked backends. It incorporates device fidelity, execution time, wait time, and communication overhead into modified graph-based allocation algorithms. Empirical evaluations report average gains of about 5% in execution time, 30% in communication overhead, 40% in wait time, and 2% in fidelity over prior approaches.
main_result: The proposed allocation methods improve multiple scheduling metrics for distributed quantum workflows on heterogeneous quantum devices.
application_angle: This is relevant to near-term quantum cloud orchestration where scarce devices must be matched to jobs efficiently and reliably.

title: Measuring the complete set of spatial Schmidt modes of entangled two-photon fields
abstract: Spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC) is the most widely-used source of high-dimensional entangled two-photon states, and the entanglement in the spatial degree of freedom is considered best suited for harnessing high-dimensional advantages. Although the Schmidt basis provides a natural choice for state characterisation of entangled two-photon states in any degree of freedom, there is currently no technique that can measure the Schmidt basis of an entangled two-photon field. The existing techniques can only reconstruct the Schmidt spectrum when the Schmidt basis is known a priori. In contrast, we present a technique that measures the complete set of spatial Schmidt modes without any prior knowledge. Using this technique, we report measurement of states with over 3000 Schmidt modes -- highest reported yet -- with up to 98$\%$ fidelity. We expect our work to significantly advance the harnessing of high-dimensional advantages in SPDC-based systems.
one_liner: A measurement method reconstructs the full spatial Schmidt-mode basis of SPDC two-photon fields without prior mode assumptions.
why_it_matters: Direct access to thousands of entangled spatial modes could make high-dimensional photonic encoding more practical and diagnosable.
detailed_summary: The work introduces a technique for measuring the complete spatial Schmidt basis of entangled two-photon SPDC fields without assuming the basis in advance. The experiment reports characterization of states containing more than 3000 Schmidt modes, with fidelity up to 98%. This addresses a key limitation of methods that only recover Schmidt spectra when the modes are already known.
main_result: The authors demonstrate measurement of over 3000 spatial Schmidt modes with up to 98% fidelity.
application_angle: The technique can support high-dimensional quantum communication, imaging, and photonic information processing by giving full modal diagnostics.

title: Probing Tensor Singularities and Their Euler-Class Descendants via Non-Abelian Quantum Geometry Measurement
abstract: We report the theoretical prediction and experimental observation of a new class of four-dimensional (4D) tensor singularities and their three-dimensional (3D) Euler-class descendants, protected by chiral and spacetime inversion symmetries on a superconducting circuit platform. The 4D point-like singularity/monopole, characterized by the Dixmier-Douady class of a real bundle gerbe associated with tensor gauge fields, is observed to evolve into a nodal ring carrying an additional first Euler class charge under symmetry-preserving perturbations. Dimensional reduction reveals 3D Euler and Euler curvature dipoles, exhibiting nontrivial Euler topology and a topological sum rule that ensures zero-energy flat bands inherit nontrivial topology even without interactions. Crucially, these high-dimensional degenerate systems are mapped and reconstructed using a hybrid analog-digital protocol designed for non-Abelian quantum geometry measurement within a superconducting qubit array. Our work not only expands the family of topological monopoles but also establishes a robust experimental framework for exploring high-order gauge theory and real-bundle topology across diverse quantum platforms.
one_liner: A superconducting-qubit platform maps 4D tensor singularities and 3D Euler descendants via non-Abelian quantum geometry measurements.
why_it_matters: It shows superconducting circuits can experimentally probe exotic high-dimensional topology and gauge structures beyond conventional band singularities.
detailed_summary: The paper predicts and observes 4D tensor singularities and 3D Euler-class descendants protected by chiral and spacetime inversion symmetries. A hybrid analog-digital measurement protocol reconstructs non-Abelian quantum geometry in a superconducting qubit array. The reported structures include a 4D point-like monopole evolving into a nodal ring with first Euler-class charge under symmetry-preserving perturbations.
main_result: The experiment reconstructs high-dimensional tensor and Euler topological structures using non-Abelian quantum geometry measurements.
application_angle: The platform provides a programmable hardware testbed for studying topological phases, gauge fields, and protected degeneracies.

title: Quantum Sidecar Architectures for Hybrid AI Training and Inference: Stateful Protected Registers, Stateless Reset-and-Reprepare Circuits and Quantum Weight-State Outlook
abstract: We propose a quantum sidecar architecture family for future hybrid AI training and inference. The central idea is not to store an entire Transformer in a small quantum memory, nor to claim one-shot collapse into a fully trained model or an optimal answer. Instead, we identify two physically distinct operating modes for quantum co-processors attached to classical large-model pipelines. The first is a stateful protected-register mode, in which a protected register stores a reusable quantum resource while an ancilla or temporary register performs QND-style readout. The second is a stateless reset-and-reprepare mode, in which each query prepares a task-conditioned quantum circuit, evolves over bounded training or inference control variables, measures candidate signals, resets the qubits, and repeats. We simulate the stateful mode using 2/4/6/8 protected-qubit density-matrix QND-style parity readout with one ancilla and a Qiskit cross-check. For the stateless mode, we include both an abstract candidate-update sampler and a circuit-level QAOA-style statevector sampler over structured candidate landscapes, followed by reset-overhead sensitivity analysis. The resulting framework positions quantum sidecars as bounded signal generators for optimizer-side sampling, adapter or expert selection, retrieval, routing, and reasoning-path proposal. As a speculative outlook, we introduce quantum weight-state sidecars: restricted quantum representations over model-control variables, not direct encodings of complete classical weight tensors.
one_liner: Quantum sidecar designs are proposed as bounded signal generators for hybrid AI workflows rather than full quantum model storage.
why_it_matters: It frames a more hardware-conscious role for quantum co-processors in AI pipelines, separating stateful protected resources from stateless sampling circuits.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes two quantum sidecar modes for classical AI training and inference pipelines: protected-register stateful operation and reset-and-reprepare stateless circuits. The stateful mode is simulated with 2, 4, 6, and 8 protected-qubit density-matrix parity readout using an ancilla, with a Qiskit cross-check. The stateless mode is studied through abstract candidate-update sampling and QAOA-style statevector sampling over structured candidate landscapes.
main_result: The work defines and simulates sidecar operating modes that generate bounded quantum signals for optimizer sampling, routing, retrieval, or expert selection.
application_angle: It targets practical integration points for future quantum accelerators in AI systems without requiring unrealistic full-model quantum memory.

title: Linear-optical test of quantum contextuality with sequential measurements
abstract: Quantum contextuality provides a fundamental signature of nonclassical behavior that cannot be explained by noncontextual hidden-variable models. We propose and experimentally implement a linear-optical setup for demonstrating Kochen-Specker contextuality via a violation of the KCBS inequality using single photons. Our scheme employs sequential measurements realized with linear-optical networks and on-off photodetectors. The construction ensures that each co-measured observable is implemented by the same physical operation across different contexts. Our experimental results demonstrate a clear violation of the KCBS inequality and robustness against photon loss. Beyond fundamental investigations, the proposed setup provides a practical tool for probing non-classicality and photon-number statistics of quantum states, which in turn enables the verification of single-photon sources.
one_liner: A single-photon linear-optical setup violates the KCBS contextuality inequality using sequential measurements.
why_it_matters: The experiment gives a practical photonic testbed for contextuality while also supporting verification of single-photon source behavior.
detailed_summary: The authors propose and experimentally implement a linear-optical network for Kochen-Specker contextuality tests with single photons. Sequential measurements and on-off photodetectors realize co-measured observables consistently across different contexts. The experiment reports a clear KCBS inequality violation with robustness against photon loss.
main_result: The setup demonstrates KCBS contextuality violation using sequential measurements implemented in linear optics.
application_angle: Beyond foundations, the method can help probe nonclassicality, photon-number statistics, and single-photon source quality.

title: Integrated time-bin entangled quantum light source on a 4H-SiC microring chip
abstract: Integrated time-bin-entangled photon-pair source with cavity-enhanced nonlinear optical processes is essential for quantum information technologies. However, microcavities with a high quality factor inherently introduce a trade-off between generation efficiency and photon bandwidth, which hinders the development of high-speed quantum networks with an integrated source. Here, we address this challenge by optimizing the nonlinearity property of the material and the geometry of the integrated microring resonator with a 4H-silicon carbide platform. Operating at a loaded quality factor of 1.9 $\times$ 10^5 - spectral bandwidth of 1.0 GHz and pumped with 300-ps double pulses separated by 1.25 ns at a repetition rate of 160 MHz, the device achieves a time-bin-entangled photon-pair generation rate of 1.35 $\times$ 10^7 s^-1 mW^-2. A raw visibility of 95.55 $\pm$ 0.18% is measured, showing a violation of Bell's inequality by more than 138 standard deviations, and a fidelity of 94.37 $\pm$ 0.22% is obtained by quantum state tomography. These results provide a scalable pathway to an efficient and broadband time-bin entangled quantum light source, overcoming intrinsic limitations of cavity-based designs and advancing integrated platforms for future quantum communication networks.
one_liner: A 4H-SiC microring chip generates high-rate, high-visibility time-bin entangled photon pairs at GHz-scale bandwidth.
why_it_matters: The result addresses the efficiency-bandwidth trade-off in cavity-enhanced integrated entangled-photon sources for high-speed quantum networks.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates an integrated time-bin-entangled photon-pair source on a 4H-silicon-carbide microring resonator. With loaded quality factor 1.9e5, 1.0 GHz bandwidth, and 160 MHz double-pulse pumping, the device reaches a generation rate of 1.35e7 s^-1 mW^-2. Measurements show 95.55 +/- 0.18% raw visibility, Bell-inequality violation by more than 138 standard deviations, and 94.37 +/- 0.22% tomography fidelity.
main_result: The chip produces broadband time-bin entangled photon pairs with high brightness, high visibility, and strong Bell violation.
application_angle: This advances scalable integrated entangled-light sources for quantum communication networks and chip-scale quantum photonics.

title: Regularized Counterdiabatic Driving for the Quantum Rabi Model
abstract: Counter-diabatic (CD) driving provides a powerful route to fast and robust state preparation by suppressing diabatic excitations during finite-time evolution. Yet, deriving analytical CD protocols for complex systems remains challenging, motivating the development of variational approaches. These methods typically rely on minimizing trace-based functionals to construct approximate control Hamiltonians. However, in unbounded systems, such functionals can become ill-defined because of the unbounded bosonic Hilbert space, leading to divergent cost functions and unphysical variational coefficients. Here, we introduce a variational optimization framework equipped with physically motivated renormalization schemes that regularize the trace-based metric by restricting it to relevant displaced and low-energy subspaces. As a paradigmatic example, we apply our method to the quantum Rabi model beyond the dispersive approximation and identify two distinct CD contributions that couple the atomic degree of freedom to the position and momentum quadratures of the field. These terms suppress diabatic excitations across coupling regimes ranging from strong to deep-strong light--matter interaction. We further formulate a fidelity-based quantum optimal-control strategy that bypasses the limitations of trace-based variational methods. Finally, we show that the resulting CD terms can be implemented via Floquet engineering through parametric modulation of the native Hamiltonian. Our results demonstrate that CD driving can be consistently extended to continuous-variable systems with unbounded Hilbert spaces, providing a controlled and scalable framework for quantum control in strongly interacting light-matter platforms.
one_liner: Regularized variational counterdiabatic controls enable fast state preparation in the quantum Rabi model across strong coupling regimes.
why_it_matters: It gives implementable control recipes for strongly interacting light-matter systems where unbounded bosonic Hilbert spaces break standard CD metrics.
detailed_summary: The paper regularizes trace-based variational counterdiabatic optimization by restricting the metric to displaced and low-energy bosonic subspaces. Applied beyond the dispersive approximation, the method finds position- and momentum-quadrature CD terms coupling the atom to the field. The authors also formulate a fidelity-based optimal-control alternative and show the CD terms can be realized with Floquet parametric modulation.
main_result: Regularized CD protocols suppress diabatic excitations from strong to deep-strong coupling in the quantum Rabi model.
application_angle: Fast, robust preparation in ultrastrong light-matter hardware is relevant to superconducting-circuit control, bosonic processors, and sensor initialization.

title: Energy-Resolved Eigenmode Spectroscopy of 1-D and 2-D Non-Hermitian Skin Effects
abstract: Non-Hermitian lattices can host the non-Hermitian skin effect, a boundary-induced collapse of all bulk eigenstates into exponentially localized edge modes. This effect underlies anomalous bulk-boundary correspondence and remarkable enhancements in non-Hermitian sensing, yet direct energy-resolved access to the eigenmodes of non-Hermitian lattices has remained limited. Here we report band- and energy-resolved eigenmode spectroscopy of skin modes in a frequency synthetic dimension. By introducing strong frequency-domain boundaries in an electro-optically modulated ring resonator, we realize finite non-Hermitian lattices and use laser detuning as a spectroscopic axis for the eigenenergies of the effective Hamiltonian. Site-resolved heterodyne measurements then reconstruct the spatial profile of each mode, revealing boundary-localized skin states throughout the spectrum and their eigenenergy-dependent displacement from the edge. Beyond 1D, the same frequency-boundary architecture, upon incorporating long-range couplings between finite lattices, produces genuine 2D frequency lattices rather than the hitherto-realized folded 1D systems on twisted tubes. In these lattices we observe tunable directional transport and edge localization in two synthetic dimensions. Our results introduce eigenmode spectroscopy as a direct probe of non-Hermitian physics and establish strongly bounded frequency lattices as a flexible platform for Hamiltonian engineering.
one_liner: Frequency-lattice spectroscopy directly images energy-resolved non-Hermitian skin modes in 1D and 2D synthetic dimensions.
why_it_matters: It provides a direct experimental handle on skin-effect eigenmodes, central to non-Hermitian photonic transport and enhanced sensing proposals.
detailed_summary: The work builds finite non-Hermitian lattices in an electro-optically modulated ring resonator using strong frequency-domain boundaries. Laser detuning resolves eigenenergies, while site-resolved heterodyne readout reconstructs each mode profile. The same architecture is extended to genuine 2D frequency lattices with tunable directional transport and edge localization.
main_result: The experiment reconstructs band- and energy-resolved skin eigenmodes in bounded 1D and 2D synthetic photonic lattices.
application_angle: Direct eigenmode access can guide non-Hermitian photonic sensors, programmable Hamiltonian simulators, and synthetic-dimension transport devices.

title: A Risk-Aware Framework for Covert Quantum Communication under Stochastic Channel Uncertainty
abstract: Covert quantum communication (CQC) seeks to hide not only message content but also the existence of communication. Existing CQC models usually assume deterministic or worst-case channel conditions, which are difficult to justify in realistic free-space optical and quantum links affected by turbulence, fluctuating background radiance, and stochastic detector noise. We propose a stochastic risk-aware optimization framework for CQC under uncertain physical-layer conditions. By modeling transmissivity and background noise as random variables, we express covertness and reliability guarantees through chance constraints with explicit outage budgets $ε_{\text{cov}}$ and $ε_{\text{rel}}$. This recasts CQC design as a risk-calibrated resource-allocation problem balancing throughput, covertness, reliability, and communication privacy. We derive quantile-based reformulations of the outage constraints, characterize feasible operating regions under stochastic uncertainty, and introduce a complementary risk-adjusted utility formulation to expose throughput-risk trade-offs. The analysis reveals that modest relaxations in acceptable covertness-outage risk can yield large throughput gains, while aggressive optimization may break covertness outside sparse-transmission regimes. Monte Carlo results under log-normal fading and stochastic thermal noise show that the framework expands feasible operating regions, improves covert throughput by more than an order of magnitude, and identifies degradation boundaries beyond which covert operation becomes unreliable. These results move CQC closer to realistic secure quantum networking for free-space, satellite, and low-probability-of-detection applications.
one_liner: A chance-constrained framework optimizes covert quantum communication under stochastic fading, background noise, and detector uncertainty.
why_it_matters: It replaces brittle worst-case assumptions with risk-calibrated design rules closer to free-space, satellite, and low-detection quantum links.
detailed_summary: The paper models transmissivity and background noise as random variables and enforces covertness and reliability through outage budgets. Quantile reformulations turn the design into a resource-allocation problem exposing throughput-risk trade-offs. Monte Carlo studies with log-normal fading and stochastic thermal noise show expanded feasible regions and more than order-of-magnitude throughput gains under calibrated risk.
main_result: Risk-aware optimization substantially increases feasible covert throughput while identifying regimes where covertness or reliability fails.
application_angle: The framework is directly relevant to satellite quantum links, low-probability-of-detection communication, and secure quantum network planning.

title: Precision limits for time-dependent quantum metrology under Markovian noise
abstract: We derive ultimate precision bounds for estimating parameters encoded in \emph{time-dependent} Hamiltonians in the presence of general Markovian noise, allowing for arbitrary adaptive protocols with fast controls and noiseless ancillas. Extending the minimization-over-purifications framework to time-varying continuous channels, we obtain a differential upper bound on the achievable quantum Fisher information (QFI) that can be evaluated at all times via semidefinite programming. For parameter-independent noise, we prove a universal long-time scaling law: if the coherent (noiseless) dynamics yields $Q_{\mathrm{coh}}(T)\sim T^{2k}$, then under Markovian noise the QFI scales at most as $Q(T)\sim T^{2k}$ in the DHNLS regime, whereas in the DHLS regime it is fundamentally limited to $Q(T)\sim T^{2k-1}$. We illustrate these behaviors on paradigmatic driven-qubit sensors, exhibiting $T^{4}$ and $T^{3}$ scalings under dephasing and spontaneous emission, respectively. Finally, we provide explicit continuous exact and approximate quantum error correction constructions -- supplemented by spin-squeezed probes -- that asymptotically saturate the bounds, establishing their tightness.
one_liner: Time-dependent metrology bounds quantify ultimate precision under Markovian noise with adaptive controls and noiseless ancillas.
why_it_matters: It tells sensor designers when error correction can recover fast coherent scaling and when noise enforces a one-power penalty in time.
detailed_summary: The authors extend purification-minimization methods to time-varying continuous channels and derive a differential semidefinite-program bound on quantum Fisher information. For parameter-independent noise, they prove long-time scaling laws separating DHNLS and DHLS regimes. Driven-qubit examples exhibit T^4 and T^3 behavior, and continuous error-correction constructions with spin-squeezed probes asymptotically saturate the bounds.
main_result: The work establishes tight QFI scaling bounds for time-dependent Hamiltonian sensing under general Markovian noise.
application_angle: These limits and saturating protocols inform practical quantum sensor design, including when ancillas, controls, and error correction are worth the hardware overhead.

title: Topologically protected long-range correlations in steady states of driven-dissipative bosonic chains
abstract: Driven-dissipative quantum systems can exhibit robust transport and amplification in topological regimes, yet the connection between topology and the extent of correlations remains largely unexplored. In this work, we develop a general framework that links topological phases in driven-dissipative systems to bosonic correlations via the singular value decomposition (SVD). In essence, we claim that non-Hermitian topology in quadratic Liouvillians is directly encoded in steady-state correlations, providing an intrinsic characterization of topology without external probes. We show that topological amplification induces disorder-robust long-range order (LRO) in steady-state correlations at fixed frequency, establishing frequency-resolved correlations as direct signatures of non-Hermitian topological phases. We introduce a vector-valued topological invariant that captures the total number of singular-value gap closings across the frequency axis, extending the concept of adiabatic deformation from topological insulators to the case of topological phases of quadratic Liouvillians. Within this framework, we further demonstrate that the spatial structure of equal-time correlations encodes global topological information, manifested as a Gaussian spatial decay with distance in the topological phase, in contrast to the exponential decay characteristic of trivial phases. These findings open new avenues for quantum sensing and correlation engineering in non-Hermitian systems, with feasible implementations in platforms such as trapped ions and superconducting circuits.
one_liner: Non-Hermitian topology is shown to imprint robust long-range steady-state correlations in driven-dissipative bosonic chains.
why_it_matters: It links measurable correlations to topological amplification, suggesting probe-free diagnostics and engineered correlation resources for quantum devices.
detailed_summary: The paper develops an SVD-based framework connecting quadratic Liouvillian topology to bosonic steady-state correlations. It shows topological amplification yields disorder-robust, frequency-resolved long-range order and introduces a vector-valued invariant counting singular-value gap closings. Equal-time correlations encode global topology through Gaussian spatial decay in the topological phase, contrasting with exponential decay in trivial phases.
main_result: Topological phases of quadratic Liouvillians are encoded directly in robust frequency-resolved and equal-time steady-state correlations.
application_angle: Correlation-based topology diagnostics and amplification could be implemented in trapped ions or superconducting circuits for sensing and engineered quantum hardware.

title: Bounds on quantum conference key agreement in pair-entangled networks
abstract: We investigate the task of conference key agreement in near-term quantum networks, where the nodes are connected by sources of bipartite entangled states, under the class of local operations not requiring quantum memory. We derive upper bounds on the distillable conference key depending on the network topology and degree of entanglement of the sources, and prove tightness of these bounds for some particular cases. In these cases, we show that pairwise bipartite key distillation followed by merging the bipartite keys into the conference key is optimal.
one_liner: Topology- and entanglement-dependent bounds are derived for conference key agreement in pair-entangled quantum networks.
why_it_matters: It clarifies when simple pairwise key distillation is already optimal for near-term multipartite quantum network security.
detailed_summary: The paper studies conference key agreement when network nodes share bipartite entangled sources and operations avoid quantum memory. It derives upper bounds on distillable conference key rates as functions of topology and source entanglement. For selected cases the bounds are tight, proving optimality of distilling bipartite keys first and then merging them into a conference key.
main_result: The authors obtain topology-sensitive upper bounds and tight optimality cases for memory-free conference key agreement.
application_angle: The results help benchmark practical secure group communication protocols in early quantum networks built from bipartite entanglement links.

title: Enhanced detection of electric field signals via squeezing-induced stochastic resonance
abstract: Stochastic resonance (SR) could amplify weak electric-field signals in nonlinear systems by means of the externally injected noises. Here we propose and experimentally demonstrate a modified SR method, termed squeezing-induced SR, implemented in the system involving a trapped ion behaving as a Duffing oscillator. We find that squeezing the phase noise of the oscillator results in amplified fluctuation of the corresponding amplitude, which helps achieve the SR. Since no auxiliary noise source is needed, the squeezing-induced SR may enhance the signal-to-noise ratio by 4.28 $\pm$ 0.39 dB compared to the conventional noise-induced SR under identical conditions of the electric-field detection. This technique offers a promising approach for developing atomic ion sensors for detecting weak electric-field signals.
one_liner: A trapped-ion Duffing oscillator uses phase-noise squeezing to boost weak electric-field detection without injecting auxiliary noise.
why_it_matters: It turns squeezing into a practical stochastic-resonance resource, improving ion-based field sensing while avoiding added-noise hardware.
detailed_summary: The work implements squeezing-induced stochastic resonance in a trapped ion operated as a nonlinear Duffing oscillator. By squeezing the oscillator phase noise, the experiment amplifies amplitude fluctuations that enhance weak electric-field readout. Compared with conventional noise-induced stochastic resonance under matched conditions, the method improves signal-to-noise ratio by 4.28 ± 0.39 dB without an external noise source.
main_result: The experiment demonstrates squeezing-induced stochastic resonance with a 4.28 ± 0.39 dB SNR gain for weak electric-field detection.
application_angle: The approach could simplify and improve atomic ion sensors for weak electric-field metrology.

title: Realization of waveguide many-body quantum optics
abstract: Controlling light photon-by-photon is central to quantum optics. At a fundamental level, photon interactions are mediated by their coupling to atoms, and ultimate control requires deterministic light-matter interfacing of single photons to single atoms. Extending this paradigm to radiatively couple multiple individual atoms in a deterministic and scalable manner opens the arena of many-body quantum optics. Here, we realize such a setting by coherently coupling solid-state artificial atoms to a nanophotonic waveguide and demonstrate higher-order photon correlations that are controlled by the number of quantum emitters. We study the scaling of nonlinear photonic transport induced by emitter-photon scattering and demonstrate that adding a quantum emitter generates higher-order photon correlations. Specifically, we experimentally observe genuine three-photon correlations from a pair of collectively coupled emitters, while contributions from lower photon numbers are suppressed. In addition, we scale to three resonant quantum emitters coupled to the waveguide. These advancements demonstrate the onset of many-body quantum optics in waveguide quantum electrodynamics, enabling new photonic quantum simulators, the creation of many-body entangled states, and the exploration of novel quantum phase transitions.
one_liner: Solid-state artificial atoms coupled to a nanophotonic waveguide generate controllable higher-order photon correlations.
why_it_matters: It shows deterministic multi-emitter waveguide QED entering a many-body regime useful for quantum simulation and photonic state engineering.
detailed_summary: The experiment coherently couples multiple solid-state artificial atoms to a nanophotonic waveguide and studies nonlinear photon transport from emitter-photon scattering. Adding emitters changes the order and structure of photon correlations. A pair of collectively coupled emitters produces genuine three-photon correlations with lower-photon-number contributions suppressed, and the platform is extended to three resonant emitters.
main_result: The work realizes waveguide many-body quantum optics and observes genuine three-photon correlations from two collectively coupled emitters.
application_angle: Scalable deterministic emitter-waveguide coupling supports photonic quantum simulators, entangled-light generation, and quantum-network components.

title: Comparative study of second harmonic generation at 1030 nm in BiBO and LBO crystals using a 100 W-class picosecond laser
abstract: We present a systematic experimental comparison of single-pass second-harmonic generation (SHG) in bismuth triborate (BiBO) and lithium triborate (LBO) nonlinear crystals, driven by a 1.3 ps, 91 kHz laser at 1030 nm with up to 57 W of average input power. Both crystals yielded 32 W of second harmonic (SH) output at 515 nm, corresponding to a conversion efficiency of 56 %, which to the best of our knowledge represents the highest SH output power reported in the green spectral region using a BiBO crystal. Power dependence, long-term stability, beam quality, pulse duration, spectral properties, thermal effects, and angular acceptance bandwidth are characterized and directly compared for both crystals. These results provide quantitative performance benchmarks to guide the selection of nonlinear crystals for high-average-power, ultrashort-pulse frequency conversion near 1030 nm.
one_liner: BiBO and LBO crystals are benchmarked for 1030-to-515 nm SHG using a 100 W-class picosecond laser.
why_it_matters: It gives practical crystal-selection data for high-average-power green ultrafast sources used in photonics and precision experiments.
detailed_summary: The paper experimentally compares single-pass second-harmonic generation in BiBO and LBO driven by 1.3 ps, 91 kHz pulses at 1030 nm with up to 57 W average input power. Both crystals reach 32 W at 515 nm with 56% conversion efficiency. The study also measures stability, beam quality, pulse duration, spectra, thermal behavior, and angular acceptance, providing side-by-side engineering benchmarks.
main_result: Both crystals deliver 32 W of 515 nm output at 56% conversion efficiency, including a reported record green output for BiBO.
application_angle: The results guide design of high-power ultrafast frequency converters for laser machining, spectroscopy, and quantum-optics setups.

title: The QuaST Decision Tree: Achieving Automation With Data-Based Recommendations
abstract: Quantum computers are increasingly powerful. Software tools for the development of quantum-enhanced algorithms are maturing. However, the software stack still lacks the connection to applications that would enable hybrid algorithms combining classical and quantum computing steps. End users need to be assisted in choosing the best combination of preprocessing, postprocessing, classical and quantum algorithms options. The application-facing software stack is therefore required to cover problem modeling, encoding, algorithm selection and hyperparameter tuning. A variety of tools exist for specific recommendations. The QuaST Decision Tree reflects the complexity in combining individual decisions in its modular network structure, consisting of flexible computation nodes with modular recommendations. It can easily be configured to serve in an industrial solver, an HPC software stack, or for rapid prototyping in development. The key ingredient, automation, is delivered by modules. We present one such module judging the feasibility of variational algorithms based on a robust scalability analysis and classification of problem instances. The automation improves the performance of end-to-end solutions, highlights the benefit to be gained from the hybrid quantum solution, reduces expensive trial-and-error testing, and leads to an improved utilization of quantum devices for a practical benefit.
one_liner: QuaST introduces a modular decision tree that automates recommendations for hybrid quantum-classical application workflows.
why_it_matters: Application-facing automation can reduce trial-and-error and help route real problem instances to viable quantum or hybrid solvers.
detailed_summary: The QuaST Decision Tree is a configurable network of computation nodes that recommends choices across modeling, encoding, algorithm selection, and tuning. The paper presents a module that judges feasibility of variational algorithms using scalability analysis and classification of problem instances. Its goal is to connect quantum devices and hybrid algorithms to industrial solvers, HPC stacks, and rapid prototyping workflows.
main_result: The work presents a modular decision-tree framework plus a feasibility module for automated variational-algorithm recommendations.
application_angle: Such tooling can improve utilization of quantum hardware by steering users toward practical workflows before expensive experiments are run.

title: Universal Jaynes-Cummings Control of an Oscillator
abstract: The Jaynes-Cummings (JC) interaction-the coherent exchange of excitations between a two-level system and a harmonic oscillator-is one of the fundamental interactions of quantum optics, realized across platforms such as cavity quantum electrodynamics, trapped ions, mechanical resonators, and superconducting circuits. Although JC interactions and qubit rotations form a universal gate set for oscillator control, practical implementations have not been demonstrated. Here we develop and experimentally demonstrate universal JC-based oscillator control by compiling arbitrary unitary gates into sequences of JC interactions and qubit rotations. In our experiment, the oscillator is realized using a mode of a high quality factor microwave cavity and the ancilla qubit using a superconducting transmon circuit, with the JC interaction implemented by a sideband interaction enabled by the Josephson nonlinearity. The native gates are constructed to be closed below a chosen cutoff photon number, encoding a qudit with suppressed leakage errors, while ancilla relaxation errors are detectable. We further find that the dispersive shift serves as a compilation resource that reduces circuit depths. We demonstrate universal qudit control and implement a single-qutrit gate set with a mean post-selected process fidelity of 96%, as well as ququart and ququint shift gates. These results establish Jaynes-Cummings control as a practical route to universal oscillator control, enabling programmable bosonic processors across a variety of quantum platforms.
one_liner: A superconducting cavity-transmon system demonstrates universal oscillator control from Jaynes-Cummings gates and qubit rotations.
why_it_matters: It makes a long-known universal control set practical for bosonic qudits, with leakage-suppressed gates and detectable ancilla errors.
detailed_summary: The authors compile arbitrary oscillator unitaries into sequences of Jaynes-Cummings interactions and qubit rotations. In experiment, a high-Q microwave cavity serves as the oscillator and a superconducting transmon as the ancilla, with sideband interactions enabled by Josephson nonlinearity. Native gates are closed below a chosen photon-number cutoff to encode qudits with suppressed leakage, and the dispersive shift reduces compiled circuit depth. The team demonstrates a single-qutrit gate set with 96% mean post-selected process fidelity plus ququart and ququint shift gates.
main_result: The experiment realizes universal JC-based oscillator control, including a qutrit gate set at 96% mean post-selected process fidelity.
application_angle: Programmable oscillator control is directly relevant to bosonic quantum processors, error-corrected encodings, and hardware-efficient qudit logic.

title: Switching Rydberg interactions by three orders of magnitude using a terahertz field
abstract: Atom-based quantum computing exploits the ability to enhance atom-atom interactions by employing laser excitation to higher-excited Rydberg states. Additional fields that drive transitions between Rydberg states can offer independent control of these atom-atom interactions. However, as microwave (mw) fields only provide access to states with similar principal quantum number $n$, their ability to switch the interactions' strength is limited. Here, we use a pulsed terahertz field to rapidly switch the strength of interactions between Rydberg atoms by three orders of magnitude. We demonstrate interaction switching using photon storage, where the terahertz field induces an interaction induced dephasing of the stored photon. This ability to switch interactions offers advantages for single-qubit readout, state-detection schemes, quantum annealing, and Rydberg quantum optics.
one_liner: A pulsed terahertz field switches Rydberg-atom interactions by three orders of magnitude during photon-storage experiments.
why_it_matters: Fast, large-range interaction control adds an independent knob for neutral-atom computing, Rydberg optics, and measurement protocols.
detailed_summary: The study uses terahertz radiation to drive transitions between Rydberg states with different interaction strengths, exceeding the tuning range available from microwave coupling between nearby principal quantum numbers. Interaction switching is demonstrated through photon storage, where the terahertz pulse induces interaction-driven dephasing of the stored photon. The reported switching range reaches three orders of magnitude.
platform_or_system: Rydberg atoms driven by terahertz fields
main_result: The experiment rapidly switches Rydberg interactions by three orders of magnitude using a pulsed terahertz field.
application_angle: Large, fast interaction tuning could improve readout, state detection, quantum annealing, and Rydberg quantum-optics architectures.

title: Strategy optimization for quantum conference key agreement in asymmetric star networks
abstract: The distribution of entangled states is a core task for quantum networks facilitating quantum communication, and the use of multipartite entangled states comes with its own set of considerations. In this work, we analyze a quantum conference agreement protocol based on GHZ states in a network with a central station to which multiple clients are connected. Using comprehensive numerical simulations, we investigate how minor variations in the scenario-such as the number of parties, the number of memories, and asymmetric distances from the central station-can drastically influence the performance of the protocol. In particular, we demonstrate that it is crucial to adjust the strategy by optimizing cutoff times. From a broader perspective, we argue that numerical simulations are an indispensable tool for protocol design for devising realistic schemes for quantum communication.
one_liner: Optimizes GHZ-based conference key agreement strategies for asymmetric star-shaped quantum networks.
why_it_matters: Shows that realistic network asymmetry and memory limits can strongly change QCKA performance, making simulation-driven protocol tuning essential.
detailed_summary: The paper studies a GHZ-state quantum conference key agreement protocol in a central-station star network with multiple clients. Numerical simulations vary party number, memory count, asymmetric link lengths, and cutoff-time strategies. The core finding is that small implementation details can drastically alter achievable rates, so cutoff optimization is not a minor parameter choice but part of the protocol design.
main_result: The work demonstrates numerically that optimized cutoff times can substantially improve QCKA performance in asymmetric star networks.
application_angle: The results guide practical design of multi-user quantum communication networks where memory resources and unequal client distances are unavoidable.

title: From order to chaos in a chip-scale Kerr parametric oscillator
abstract: Integrated photonics has enabled a wide class of chip-scale light sources and quantum technologies. Within this field, microresonator-based degenerate optical parametric oscillators (DOPOs) have gained prominence. Above a critical power threshold, these systems undergo spontaneous symmetry breaking to settle into one of two stable, π-phase-shifted states -- a mechanism successfully used for quantum random number generation and photonic Ising machines. Here, we show that DOPOs based on the Kerr nonlinearity host a significantly broader range of nonlinear dynamics than previously explored. Using a silicon nitride microring resonator, we experimentally identify Hopf bifurcations that trigger a transition from stationary operation to self-sustained oscillations at MHz frequencies. By adjusting pump detunings and powers, we achieve turnkey control over these oscillatory regimes, navigating the system between stable binary states and periodic limit cycles. Furthermore, we report the experimental observation of period-doubling bifurcations, which numerical simulations reveal as the precursor to a cascading instability culminating in chaos at elevated pump powers. Our results establish a framework for controlling nonlinear instabilities in chip-scale parametric oscillators, with applications in programmable photonic hardware and dynamical optical computing.
one_liner: A silicon nitride Kerr DOPO is driven from stable binary operation through oscillations toward chaos on chip.
why_it_matters: Controlled nonlinear regimes in chip parametric oscillators expand their use beyond binary states toward programmable photonic dynamics and computing.
detailed_summary: The authors experimentally probe a microresonator-based degenerate optical parametric oscillator using a silicon nitride microring. By tuning pump detunings and powers, they access stable phase-binary states, MHz self-sustained oscillations from Hopf bifurcations, and period-doubling behavior. Simulations connect the observed period doubling to a cascade toward chaos at higher pump powers.
main_result: The experiment demonstrates turnkey control of stationary, limit-cycle, period-doubled, and chaos-precursor regimes in a chip-scale Kerr parametric oscillator.
application_angle: This enables reconfigurable nonlinear photonic hardware for random generation, Ising machines, and dynamical optical computing.

title: Detecting nonclassicality in randomly-displaced copies of a squeezed state
abstract: We address a fundamental question: Can one determine whether a received signal is squeezed when each copy arrives with a different displacement/amplitude? We introduce an interaction Hamiltonian that converts quadrature squeezing into number squeezing. Using this conversion, we test whether the copies satisfy $g^{(2)}(0)<1$. The Hamiltonian itself does not create nonclassicality; it only transfers it from quadrature squeezing to number squeezing. This allows us to identify squeezing even when individual copies have random displacements.
one_liner: Proposes detecting squeezed-state nonclassicality even when each copy has a random displacement.
why_it_matters: It addresses a practical receiver problem where amplitude fluctuations can hide quadrature squeezing in optical or microwave quantum signals.
detailed_summary: The paper asks whether squeezing can be certified when nominally identical copies arrive with different unknown displacements. It introduces an interaction Hamiltonian that maps quadrature squeezing into number squeezing without generating new nonclassicality. The proposed test then checks for sub-Poissonian statistics through g^(2)(0)<1, making the squeezing witness robust to random amplitudes.
main_result: The authors show theoretically that quadrature squeezing can be converted into detectable number squeezing for nonclassicality tests under random displacements.
application_angle: The method could improve quantum optical signal verification in channels or sources with uncontrolled amplitude noise.

title: Detecting vortex motion through spatially correlated nonequilibrium noise
abstract: Resistive transport near a superconducting phase can arise from the motion of normal-state quasiparticles or that of vortices. The conductivity alone does not distinguish between these mechanisms. We propose an unambiguous method for telling them apart, using the recently developed experimental tool of covariance magnetometry, which uses nitrogen-vacancy centers in diamond to probe real-time spatiotemporal correlations in magnetic noise.
