Our key insight is that, under an applied current, the underlying charge carriers leave a directional fingerprint in the spatially correlated magnetic noise above the sample: ordinary electric carriers drift parallel to the current, whereas vortices, owing to the Magnus force, drift perpendicular to it. The noise covariance detects this anisotropy and identifies the vortex-driven nature of transport. We compute the noise correlations expected for a representative thin-film superconductor and demonstrate that the anisotropic signal is well within the reach of current experimental capabilities.
one_liner: Uses NV covariance magnetometry to distinguish vortex-driven transport from quasiparticle drift in superconductors.
why_it_matters: It provides a spatial noise fingerprint for vortex motion, resolving an ambiguity that ordinary conductivity measurements cannot address.
detailed_summary: The authors propose using nitrogen-vacancy centers in diamond to measure real-time spatial correlations of magnetic noise above a current-biased superconducting sample. The key signature is directional: normal carriers drift along the applied current, while vortices drift perpendicular to it because of the Magnus force. Calculations for representative thin-film superconductors indicate that the anisotropic covariance signal should be measurable with current experimental capabilities.
main_result: The work predicts a measurable anisotropic magnetic-noise covariance that identifies vortex-mediated resistive transport.
application_angle: This offers a practical diagnostic for superconducting materials, devices, and fluctuation regimes where transport mechanisms are otherwise ambiguous.

title: Generalized Hydrodynamics of Bloch Oscillations in the Absence of a Lattice
abstract: Objects subjected to a constant force generally increase their velocity over time. This expectation fails whenever their energy is a smooth and periodic function of momentum, resulting in periodic Bloch oscillations instead. Periodic dispersions, typical of lattice systems, can also emerge in continuum media through strong interactions. Here, we study the phenomenon of such Bloch oscillations in the absence of a lattice in a paradigmatic model of integrable quantum gases: the two-component Yang-Gaudin model. We derive a generalized-hydrodynamic theory of Bloch oscillations for a finite density of impurities embedded in a homogeneous interacting background, which we show to persist superimposed to a drift due to the acceleration of the center of mass. Moreover, we show the single-impurity oscillation period is renormalized at finite impurity density when two-magnon bound states are populated. Our results are relevant for ultracold atom experiments, where impurities can be created at controllable densities.
one_liner: Develops generalized hydrodynamics for Bloch oscillations of impurities in a lattice-free interacting quantum gas.
why_it_matters: It connects transport oscillations usually tied to lattices with continuum ultracold gases, giving experiments a route to probe interaction-induced periodic dispersion.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes Bloch oscillations without an external lattice in the two-component Yang-Gaudin model. Strong interactions create an effective periodic momentum dispersion for impurities embedded in a homogeneous background. The generalized-hydrodynamic treatment predicts oscillations overlaid with center-of-mass drift and shows that finite impurity density can renormalize the oscillation period when two-magnon bound states are occupied.
main_result: The authors derive a finite-density hydrodynamic theory predicting lattice-free Bloch oscillations and density-dependent period renormalization.
application_angle: The predictions are testable with controllable impurity densities in ultracold atom experiments studying interaction-driven transport.

title: Scalable Single-Step Generation of W States in 2D Superconducting Qubit Lattices
abstract: The reliable generation of multi-qubit entanglement is a prerequisite for large-scale quantum information technologies. In particular, W states are a valuable resource owing to their resilience under local loss or measurement. Nevertheless, preparing these states with sequential two-qubit gates often requires substantial time overhead. By contrast, engineered simultaneous interactions enable fast entanglement generation, even in qubit systems with limited nearest-neighbour connectivity. Here, we demonstrate a set of fast and robust operations for coherently distributing a single excitation across a lattice of arbitrary size, thereby directly generating W states from initial product states. In 2D lattices, the excitation propagates along both directions simultaneously, such that the total entanglement time scales only with the largest dimension. We exploit this property to prepare a six-qubit W state in a 3$\times$2 superconducting lattice within 99 ns, achieving a tomographic fidelity of 83.9$\pm$1.0%. We then extend the protocol to create entanglement across chains of up to seven qubits, with the largest W state generated in 264 ns with a fidelity of 79.6$\pm$1.3%.
one_liner: Demonstrates fast single-step W-state generation across 2D superconducting qubit lattices.
why_it_matters: Parallel engineered interactions reduce entanglement time versus gate sequences, supporting scalable preparation of loss-resilient multipartite states.
detailed_summary: The authors engineer simultaneous interactions that coherently spread a single excitation over superconducting qubit arrays. In 2D, propagation occurs along both lattice directions, so preparation time scales with the largest array dimension rather than total qubit number. They prepare a six-qubit W state on a 3x2 lattice in 99 ns with 83.9% tomographic fidelity and extend the method to chains of up to seven qubits.
main_result: The experiment generates a six-qubit W state in 99 ns at 83.9±1.0% fidelity and a seven-qubit chain W state in 264 ns at 79.6±1.3% fidelity.
application_angle: The protocol provides a fast hardware-native route to multipartite entanglement for quantum processors with nearest-neighbor connectivity.

title: Signatures of Gaussian superconducting fluctuations in nonlocal noise magnetometry
abstract: We calculate the two-point magnetic noise spectrum arising from Gaussian superconducting fluctuations, a quantity directly measurable by spin qubit pairs such as nitrogen vacancy centers in diamond. The analysis utilizes the time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau theory, reflecting the direct contribution of fluctuating Cooper pairs to the current correlations and consequent magnetic noise. We treat both two-dimensional systems and wires, considering them in equilibrium and under a uniform electric field. The signal is expected to be strongest in high-temperature superconductors, and we contrast our findings with the predicted signatures of a vortex liquid to offer an additional route to elucidate the nature of fluctuations in these systems.
one_liner: Calculates nonlocal magnetic noise from Gaussian superconducting fluctuations measurable by paired spin-qubit magnetometers.
why_it_matters: It gives NV-center-style noise magnetometry a concrete diagnostic for distinguishing Cooper-pair fluctuations from vortex-liquid behavior in superconductors.
detailed_summary: The paper derives two-point magnetic noise spectra generated by Gaussian superconducting fluctuations using time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau theory. It treats two-dimensional films and wires, both in equilibrium and under uniform electric fields, and connects fluctuating Cooper-pair current correlations to magnetic noise measurable by spin-qubit pairs. The authors contrast the predicted signatures with vortex-liquid noise to help identify the microscopic origin of fluctuation regimes.
main_result: The work predicts measurable nonlocal magnetic-noise signatures of Gaussian superconducting fluctuations in 2D systems and wires.
application_angle: The result supports nanoscale, noninvasive probes of high-temperature superconducting fluctuation physics using paired quantum sensors.

title: Efficient Fourier-Based Linear Combination of Unitaries and Applications in Quantum Optimization
abstract: We investigate ancilla-free linear combination of unitaries (LCU) as a framework for approximating complex quantum circuits. This is particularly effective for quantum optimization algorithms, where candidate solutions can be evaluated classically and the task is to sample high-quality bitstrings rather than reproduce the full output distribution. We show that Fourier-based LCU constructions efficiently decompose broad classes of diagonal and non-diagonal unitaries, replacing highly connected qubit interactions with single-qubit gate layers or significantly simpler structures at the cost of a polynomial sampling overhead. Applied to algorithms such as QAOA, this yields efficient, hardware-friendly decompositions of, for instance, cardinality-constraint penalties and the fully connected XY-mixer, while maintaining rigorous performance guarantees compared to fully coherent implementations. Furthermore, we establish a formal connection between Fourier-based quantum penalties and Lagrangian relaxation, offering a unified perspective on constraint handling. We validate our approach using exact statevector simulations of 12-qubit circuits and large-scale experiments on 106 superconducting qubits. Our results illustrate how approximate sampling via an LCU systematically trades circuit complexity for sampling overhead, extending the practical reach of near-term quantum optimization.
one_liner: Introduces Fourier-based ancilla-free LCU decompositions that simplify quantum optimization circuits with controlled sampling overhead.
why_it_matters: It shows a practical route to trade coherent circuit depth and connectivity for more sampling, including experiments on 106 superconducting qubits.
detailed_summary: The paper develops Fourier-based linear-combination-of-unitaries constructions for diagonal and non-diagonal unitaries without ancillas. The approach replaces highly connected interactions, such as cardinality penalties and fully connected XY mixers, with simpler gate structures while preserving performance guarantees relative to coherent implementations. It is validated with 12-qubit statevector simulations and large-scale experiments on 106 superconducting qubits.
main_result: The authors demonstrate hardware-friendly approximate LCU implementations of optimization primitives with polynomial sampling overhead.
application_angle: This can extend near-term quantum optimization to denser constraints and mixers than hardware connectivity would otherwise permit.

title: Collective charge measurement in quantum dot chains: controlling barrier occupation and tunneling current
abstract: We investigate nonequilibrium transport in a triple-quantum-dot (TQD) system, where the central dot acts as a discrete tunnel barrier, subject to continuous monitoring by a quantum point contact (QPC) that is capacitively coupled to all three dots with independently tunable strengths. We show that this global measurement scheme affects transport in a qualitatively distinct manner from single-site measurement. By engineering structured dephasing, measurement provides a significant improvement in the barrier occupation and tunneling current. In the strong-measurement limit, the steady state becomes independent of the underlying Hamiltonian parameters, and the barrier occupation can approach 1/2 for suitable measurement configurations. We identify an optimal measurement configuration that maximizes the steady-state current and show that near-optimal performance can be achieved with a simple central-dot readout scheme.
one_liner: Shows that collective QPC charge measurement can enhance barrier occupation and current in a monitored triple-quantum-dot chain.
why_it_matters: It turns measurement backaction into a tunable transport resource, relevant for engineered readout and control in quantum-dot devices.
detailed_summary: The work studies nonequilibrium transport through a triple-quantum-dot system where the central dot acts as a discrete tunnel barrier. A quantum point contact continuously monitors all three dots with independently tunable capacitive couplings, producing structured dephasing distinct from single-site measurement. Suitable measurement configurations enhance central-barrier occupation and steady-state tunneling current, with a strong-measurement limit approaching barrier occupation of one half.
main_result: The paper identifies collective measurement settings that maximize steady-state current and can be approximated by central-dot readout.
application_angle: The findings inform measurement-assisted transport control in semiconductor quantum-dot circuits and charge-sensing architectures.

title: Dominant vibronic relaxation channels in a europium-based molecular qubit
abstract: Molecular spin qubits offer a versatile platform for quantum information processing due to their synthetic tunability and well-defined electronic structure. Here, a fitted-parameter-free computational framework combining density functional theory (DFT), time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT), and Redfield theory is applied to investigate the longitudinal spin-lattice relaxation time $T_1$ of the Eu nuclear spin qubit Eu(dpphen)(NO3)3. Using a single-molecule gas-phase model, the experimental long relaxation component $T_{1,\mathrm{long}} = 41.39$ s is reproduced within a factor of 1.4 (calculated: 55.88 s at 4.2 K), indicating that the slow relaxation channel is governed by intramolecular vibronic coupling. In contrast, the calculated $T_{1,\mathrm{short}}$ deviates by a factor of 66, highlighting the importance of crystal lattice and intermolecular effects absent from the model. The experimental $^5D_0 \rightarrow {}^7F_0$ optical transition is reproduced to within 1.1%, supporting the accuracy of the electronic structure description. Vibrational analysis identifies a large-amplitude dpphen rocking mode at a frequency of $332.02~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ as the dominant vibronic coupling channel, while electric field gradient (EFG) derivative analysis independently identifies another nitrate-rocking mode at $180.57~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ as the primary modulator of the nuclear spin environment via nitrate motion. These results are consistent with a near-maximal quadrupole asymmetry parameter $η= 0.941$, which creates state mixing through off-diagonal quadrupolar terms. Overall, the results establish a single-molecule relaxation baseline and suggest targeted ligand rigidification and substitution strategies to suppress decoherence.
one_liner: Identifies dominant intramolecular vibronic relaxation channels limiting a europium molecular nuclear-spin qubit.
why_it_matters: It links specific ligand and nitrate motions to spin-lattice relaxation, giving chemists concrete design targets for longer-lived molecular qubits.
detailed_summary: The paper combines DFT, TD-DFT, and Redfield theory without fitted parameters to model T1 relaxation in Eu(dpphen)(NO3)3. The single-molecule gas-phase model reproduces the long experimental relaxation component within a factor of 1.4 at 4.2 K but misses the short component, indicating missing lattice or intermolecular effects. Vibrational and electric-field-gradient analyses identify dpphen rocking and nitrate rocking modes as key relaxation channels tied to quadrupolar state mixing.
main_result: The study establishes a single-molecule relaxation baseline and pinpoints vibronic modes that dominate long-component T1 decay.
application_angle: It suggests ligand rigidification and substitution strategies for chemically engineering molecular qubits with reduced decoherence.

title: Signatures of quantum noise in the operation of Deutsch's algorithm
abstract: We use Deutsch's algorithm as a stand in for more complex quantum algorithms in order to determine how quantum properties of an environment manifest themselves in results that can be obtained on quantum computers. We model pure dephasing in two different ways; one keeps the full density matrix of the qubits and environments (quantum) while the other uses Kraus operators (classical). We find that a single run of the algorithm yields the same effect in both cases, but running the algorithm twice leads to stark differences. Taking correlations and interplay between different decoherence processes into account leads to a slowing of decoherence effects for balanced functions. For constant functions, the effect is much more pronounced, and there is a qualitative change in the dependence of measurement outcomes on decoherence. We present results obtained on one of the IBM Quantum processors, which fully reproduce the predicted effect regardless of the assumptions made in the derivation. We further illustrate the findings on NV center spin qubits, which show more complex behavior due to a small size of the environment.
one_liner: Uses repeated Deutsch-algorithm runs to expose quantum-environment noise signatures on IBM processors and NV-center models.
why_it_matters: It shows that temporal correlations in quantum noise can alter algorithm outputs in ways hidden by single-run decoherence models.
detailed_summary: The authors compare pure dephasing modeled with a full quantum environment against a Kraus-operator classical-noise description using Deutsch's algorithm. A single run produces the same effect in both pictures, but two consecutive runs reveal strong differences due to correlations and interplay among decoherence processes. IBM Quantum experiments reproduce the predicted behavior, while NV-center spin-qubit simulations show richer dynamics from a small environment.
platform_or_system: IBM superconducting qubits and NV-center spin qubits
main_result: The paper demonstrates that repeated algorithm execution can reveal signatures of quantum, temporally correlated noise.
application_angle: The method provides a compact diagnostic for characterizing nonclassical noise effects in quantum processors and spin-qubit platforms.

title: Quantum Koopman Algorithms
abstract: We define an observable-space framework of Quantum Koopman Algorithms (QKAs) for simulating the dynamics of both linear quantum and nonlinear classical systems, based on approximately closed sets of observables and efficient coherent encodings of their Koopman-driven evolution. QKAs have two strands: Dynamic-QKA for the initial-value problem of observables dynamics, and Spectral-QKA for the eigenvalue analysis of the Koopman operator. We demonstrate the scope of the framework through several applications. First, for classes of $N$ free fermions linearly coupled to a bath, we construct quantum algorithms with gate cost $O(\mathrm{polylog}(N))$, an exponential improvement over classical methods, and use them to reconstruct heat flows and decay rates. Second, for nonlinear classical dynamics, we introduce a novel nonlinear interaction-picture quantum algorithm that enables perturbative expansions around solvable nonlinear reference flows, going beyond existing approaches that only apply to weakly nonlinear systems. Third, we develop spectral methods for extracting eigen-frequencies of late-time nonlinear dynamics, introducing a windowed quantum ODE-solver. Our results identify the Koopman-quantum interface as a natural setting in which quantum algorithms can exploit observable-space structure to simulate both classical and quantum dynamics.
one_liner: Defines Quantum Koopman Algorithms for observable-space simulation of linear quantum and nonlinear classical dynamics.
why_it_matters: It broadens quantum simulation beyond state-space evolution by exploiting approximately closed observables and Koopman spectral structure.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces Dynamic-QKA for initial-value observable dynamics and Spectral-QKA for Koopman eigenvalue analysis. For classes of free fermions linearly coupled to a bath, it gives quantum algorithms with polylogarithmic gate cost in system size and applies them to heat flows and decay rates. It also develops a nonlinear interaction-picture method for perturbations around solvable nonlinear flows and a windowed quantum ODE solver for late-time spectral extraction.
main_result: The work provides a Koopman-based quantum algorithmic framework for simulating selected quantum and nonlinear classical dynamics efficiently.
application_angle: The framework could aid sensor modeling, transport analysis, and dynamical-system spectroscopy where relevant observables close under evolution.

title: Quantum Magic Reveals CP Phases Invisible to Entanglement in Spin-0 Decays
abstract: All standard scalar quantum-information measures -- concurrence, negativity, entanglement entropy, the optimized CHSH bound, and quantum Fisher information -- are CP-blind in ideal \\ spin-0 $\to f\bar f$ decays because the two-qubit spin state is maximally entangled for every CP angle. We show that stabilizer magic, fixed in the physical Pauli frame of spin analysis, escapes this blind spot: the stabilizer Rényi entropy admits an exact closed form, vanishing at CP-definite and Clifford phases and peaking at maximal non-Clifford mixing. Two experimentally accessible, magic-inspired CP witnesses follow; the linear amplitude is $14.3\times$ more efficient than its quartic counterpart and reaches discovery-level sensitivity at the HL-LHC for $H\toτ^+τ^-$.
one_liner: Stabilizer magic exposes CP phases in spin-0 fermion decays that standard entanglement and QFI measures miss.
why_it_matters: It proposes experimentally accessible CP witnesses for Higgs and related decays, with projected HL-LHC sensitivity in H to tau tau.
detailed_summary: The paper shows that common two-qubit quantum information measures remain invariant with CP angle in ideal spin-0 to fermion-antifermion decays because the spin state stays maximally entangled. By fixing the physical Pauli frame of spin analysis, stabilizer Renyi entropy becomes CP-sensitive, with a closed-form dependence that vanishes at CP-definite and Clifford phases. Two magic-inspired CP witnesses are derived, including a linear-amplitude witness that is much more statistically efficient than a quartic alternative.
main_result: Stabilizer magic provides CP-sensitive observables invisible to entanglement-based diagnostics and can reach discovery-level HL-LHC sensitivity for H to tau tau.
application_angle: The method could improve practical CP-phase searches in collider data using quantum-information observables tied to measurable spin correlations.

title: Unitary discretization of the Koopman-von Neumann equation for quantum simulation of fluid and plasma dynamics
abstract: The Koopman--von Neumann (KvN) formulation of spectrally truncated fluid and plasma dynamics is considered as a potential approach for quantum computation. The KvN framework embeds the Liouville equation into a Hilbert space with norm-preserving, unitary evolution. Here, we propose a Weyl-ordered KvN generator along with a summation-by-parts discretization, which ensures that the resulting operators are exactly unitary as required for quantum computers. The Weyl-ordered KvN generator is derived as the unique anti-Hermitian operator symmetrization for real velocity fields. The formulation operates directly in the physical amplitude space without phase-space doubling, so the Heisenberg uncertainty principle does not constrain the grid resolution during evolution. This limitation re-enters only at the measurement stage on a quantum computer. Exact discrete unitarity is proved as a purely algebraic identity that holds regardless of grid resolution or stencil order. To manage boundaries, a split-step Kraus absorbing layer is introduced via a Stinespring dilation requiring only one ancilla qubit. Validation on three test cases spanning dissipative and Hamiltonian regimes (a viscous Navier--Stokes triad, an incompressible Euler triad, and a Hasegawa--Mima drift-wave triad) confirms fourth-order convergence and machine-precision unitarity.
one_liner: A unitary KvN discretization enables quantum-simulation-ready fluid and plasma dynamics without phase-space doubling.
why_it_matters: Exact discrete unitarity and validated convergence make the formulation relevant to future quantum algorithms for continuum physics.
detailed_summary: The work formulates spectrally truncated fluid and plasma dynamics in a Koopman-von Neumann Hilbert-space representation with norm-preserving evolution. A Weyl-ordered generator and summation-by-parts discretization give exactly anti-Hermitian discrete operators, guaranteeing unitarity algebraically for any grid resolution or stencil order. Boundary absorption is handled through a split-step Kraus layer implemented by a one-ancilla Stinespring dilation, and tests on Navier-Stokes, Euler, and Hasegawa-Mima triads show fourth-order convergence with machine-precision unitarity.
main_result: The paper proves and validates an exactly unitary discretization for KvN quantum simulation of fluid and plasma models.
application_angle: It supplies a hardware-compatible route to quantum algorithms for high-dimensional fluid, plasma, and sensing-adjacent field dynamics.

title: Quantum Accreditation with Non-Clifford Two-qubit Gates
abstract: We develop a family of quantum accreditation protocols for quantum circuits with non-Clifford two-qubit gates. The latter includes families of gates such as the fSim and XY families of gates, native to existing hardwares. We provide practical and scalable protocols that upper-bound the total variation distance between the probability distributions of error-free and erroneous quantum computations. We also establish the robustness of our protocols to small perturbations and generalize Pauli twirling to non-Pauli single-qubit bases, which may be of independent interest.
one_liner: New accreditation protocols certify circuits containing native non-Clifford two-qubit gates such as fSim and XY operations.
why_it_matters: Verification methods that handle native entangling gates are important for scalable trust in near-term and fault-tolerant hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper develops scalable quantum accreditation protocols for circuits that include non-Clifford two-qubit gate families native to current devices. The protocols upper-bound the total variation distance between ideal and erroneous output distributions, giving a direct operational certification metric. The authors also analyze robustness to small perturbations and extend Pauli twirling ideas to non-Pauli single-qubit bases.
main_result: The work provides practical accreditation protocols for quantum circuits using non-Clifford two-qubit gates including fSim and XY families.
application_angle: It supports more realistic device certification without compiling away hardware-native entangling gates.

title: Entangling gate performance and fidelity limits with neutral atom Förster resonances
abstract: Neutral-atom entangling gates are commonly analyzed with a single effective Rydberg-pair state, but near Förster resonances the pair manifold contains resonantly coupled interaction channels that change both the control landscape and the achievable fidelity. We develop a two-eigenstate model for this regime and show that when allowing for coupling to both pair states in the resonance, the gate fidelity is bounded by $\mathcal{F}\leq 1-(π/2)/(Vτ_R)$, for interaction strength $V$ and Rydberg lifetime $τ_R$. We construct a gate protocol that saturates this bound in the large-Rabi-frequency limit, improving the existing fidelity limit by approximately $40\%$. We also evaluate common gate protocols near Förster resonances and find that retaining the exchange dynamics increases predicted fidelities by up to two orders of magnitude over earlier treatments.
one_liner: A two-eigenstate Förster-resonance model tightens neutral-atom entangling-gate fidelity limits and improves protocols.
why_it_matters: Accurate modeling near Förster resonances can materially raise predicted and achievable neutral-atom gate fidelities.
detailed_summary: The paper revisits neutral-atom entangling gates near Förster resonances, where a single effective Rydberg-pair state misses resonantly coupled interaction channels. A two-eigenstate model yields a fidelity bound of F <= 1 - (pi/2)/(V tau_R) and identifies a protocol that saturates it in the large-Rabi-frequency limit. Including exchange dynamics changes the control landscape and can increase predicted fidelities for common gate protocols by up to two orders of magnitude relative to earlier simplified treatments.
main_result: The authors derive a Förster-resonance fidelity limit and a gate protocol that improves the previous limit by about 40 percent.
application_angle: The result gives neutral-atom hardware teams a sharper design rule for high-fidelity entangling gates near resonant Rydberg interactions.

title: High-stability offset-frequency locking of two lasers using a balanced filter discriminator
abstract: We demonstrate a high-stability laser offset-frequency locking technique based on a balanced filter discriminator. The beat note between two 852 nm external-cavity diode lasers is down-converted in two parallel arms using local-oscillator frequencies placed symmetrically around the desired offset frequency. After low-pass filtering and RMS detection, differential subtraction of the two detector outputs produces a dispersive frequency-error signal with a zero crossing primarily defined by the reference local-oscillator frequencies. This balanced configuration reduces sensitivity to common beat-power fluctuations and can improve the effective error-signal signal-to-noise ratio. The system was implemented for an 8.653 GHz offset corresponding to the cesium repumping frequency difference used in our laser-cooling setup. Measurements with different low-pass filters reveal a trade-off between discrimination sensitivity and feedback bandwidth. With an SLP-1.9+ filter, the locked beat frequency reached a fractional instability of $4\times10^{-15}$ at 10 s when referred to the 852 nm optical carrier. The residual dependence on photodetector optical power was also characterized, showing that amplitude-to-frequency conversion remains small in the optimized differential configuration. This approach provides a practical frequency-only offset-locking method for atomic-physics experiments requiring stable and tunable microwave-scale laser frequency offsets.
one_liner: A balanced filter discriminator stabilizes an 8.653 GHz laser offset lock between two 852 nm diode lasers.
why_it_matters: Compact, frequency-only offset locking with 4e-15 fractional instability is useful for atomic experiments needing stable microwave-scale laser offsets.
detailed_summary: The experiment locks the beat note of two 852 nm external-cavity diode lasers using two down-conversion arms with local oscillators symmetrically placed around the target offset. Low-pass filtering, RMS detection, and differential subtraction generate a dispersive error signal whose zero crossing is set mainly by the RF references while suppressing common beat-power noise. Implemented at an 8.653 GHz cesium repumping offset, the system reaches 4e-15 fractional instability at 10 s relative to the optical carrier with an SLP-1.9+ filter.
main_result: The demonstrated balanced discriminator lock achieves 4e-15 fractional instability at 10 s for an 8.653 GHz optical beat offset.
application_angle: It offers a practical, tunable laser-frequency-control method for laser cooling, atom interferometry, clocks, and related photonic experiments.

title: Ultrafast Nano-Imaging and Optical Control of Hyperbolic Phonon Polaritons at hBN/WS$_2$ Heterojunctions
abstract: Manipulating nanoscale light-matter interactions on ultrafast time scales is indispensable for future polaritonic devices. Hyperbolic phonon polaritons (HPhPs) in van der Waals materials enable deep subwavelength confinement of electromagnetic fields in the infrared region and long-distance propagation of polaritonic waves. However, achieving ultrafast imaging and optical control of HPhPs remains a major challenge. Here, we demonstrate the direct observation of transient modulation of HPhPs induced by local photocarrier generation in WS$_2$/hBN heterostructures using ultrafast infrared scanning near-field optical microscopy. We implement grating-based spectral filtering of broadband near-field scattering to simultaneously achieve nanoscale and femtosecond spatiotemporal resolution together with fine spectral selectivity. This ultrafast nano-imaging technique reveals that photocarriers in WS$_2$ modulate the polaritonic field amplitudes and wavelengths of HPhPs in hBN. Theoretical simulations corroborate that these changes arise from photoinduced changes in WS$_2$ dielectric properties. This approach offers a versatile platform for exploring ultrafast polaritonic dynamics at the nanoscale.
one_liner: Ultrafast near-field microscopy directly images optical control of hBN hyperbolic phonon polaritons by WS2 photocarriers.
why_it_matters: Femtosecond nanoscale control of polaritons is a key capability for active infrared nanophotonic and van der Waals devices.
detailed_summary: The study uses ultrafast infrared scanning near-field optical microscopy to observe transient modulation of hyperbolic phonon polaritons in WS2/hBN heterostructures. Grating-based spectral filtering of broadband near-field scattering provides simultaneous nanoscale spatial resolution, femtosecond timing, and spectral selectivity. The measurements show that photo-generated carriers in WS2 alter hBN polariton amplitudes and wavelengths, consistent with simulations based on photoinduced changes to the WS2 dielectric response.
main_result: The paper demonstrates direct femtosecond nano-imaging and optical modulation of hBN hyperbolic phonon polaritons via WS2 photocarriers.
application_angle: The platform points toward ultrafast, actively tunable infrared polaritonic components for nanoscale photonics and sensing.

title: Exact dynamics of a single-photon emitter in front of a mirror
abstract: Single-photon emitters in nanophotonic structures are a key building block for many photonic devices with quantum technology applications, like quantum sensors and quantum computers. In this paper, we determine the exact dynamics of a single-photon emitter in a one-dimensional waveguide terminated by a partially-transparent mirror interface, by solving the Schrodinger equation via a local-photon approach. In general, the evolution of the emitter is non-Markovian, characterized by a non-exponential decay profile. The decay can resemble an exponential after a time that is much larger than the emitter-mirror round-trip time and becomes exponential in the Markovian limit, where the round-trip time between the emitter and the mirror is neglected. We also derive the spatial and spectral profile of the emitted photon wave packet and demonstrate how its properties are altered by the environment.
one_liner: Exact waveguide-QED dynamics show how a partially transparent mirror reshapes single-emitter decay and photon wave packets.
why_it_matters: Accurate non-Markovian emitter modeling is important for designing nanophotonic single-photon sources, sensors, and feedback-based quantum devices.
detailed_summary: The paper solves the Schrodinger dynamics of a single-photon emitter coupled to a one-dimensional waveguide terminated by a partially transparent mirror. Using a local-photon approach, it captures delayed feedback and non-exponential decay outside the Markovian limit. It also derives emitted-photon spatial and spectral profiles, showing how mirror transmissivity and emitter distance modify the output wave packet.
main_result: The work provides exact emitter decay and emitted-photon profiles for a mirror-terminated 1D waveguide, including non-Markovian feedback effects.
application_angle: The model can guide engineering of single-photon sources and nanophotonic environments for quantum sensing and photonic quantum hardware.

title: Precision probing of ionic-core transitions in alkaline-earth Rydberg atoms
abstract: We report precision spectroscopy of ionic-core transitions in alkaline-earth Rydberg atoms. We demonstrate high-resolution measurements of isotope shifts and hyperfine splitting of dipole transitions in ionic cores which have not been explored so far. A key element of this work is the reduction of the linewidth by more than two orders of magnitude enabled by dynamical control of Rydberg electron's orbit which significantly enhances the spectral resolution. Furthermore, to unambiguously identify the frequency shift, we directly compare core ion's spectrum with a signal from a single trapped ion serving as an ultimate frequency reference. This work provides an important foundation for quantum control of inner-core transitions, which offer an useful tool in manipulating Rydberg atom as well as a sensitive probe for electron-core interactions in atomic and molecular systems.
one_liner: Precision spectroscopy resolves ionic-core isotope shifts and hyperfine structure inside alkaline-earth Rydberg atoms.
why_it_matters: Narrow, controllable core transitions add a new handle for Rydberg quantum control and precision probes of electron-core interactions.
detailed_summary: The authors perform high-resolution spectroscopy of previously unexplored dipole transitions in the ionic cores of alkaline-earth Rydberg atoms. Dynamical control of the Rydberg electron orbit reduces linewidth by more than two orders of magnitude, enabling precision isotope-shift and hyperfine measurements. They benchmark the observed shifts against a single trapped ion used as a direct frequency reference.
main_result: The experiment demonstrates narrow-line probing of ionic-core transitions in Rydberg atoms with direct trapped-ion frequency comparison.
application_angle: Core-transition control could improve Rydberg-atom manipulation and provide sensitive diagnostics for quantum processors and atomic-structure studies.

title: Efficient Fault-Tolerant Ancilla Preparation for Quantum BCH codes via Cyclic Symmetry
abstract: One of the major challenges in realizing fault-tolerant quantum computers (FTQCs) is the requirement for a large number of physical qubits. To address this issue, high-rate quantum error correcting codes, which efficiently embed logical qubits into physical qubits, have recently attracted considerable attention. Among such codes, quantum BCH codes, which offer both high rates and large code distances, are promising yet underexplored candidates. However, no fault-tolerant ancilla preparation method specialized for this class had been established. We employ a two-stage approach (non-fault-tolerant preparation + entanglement distillation) for ancilla preparation. We then propose a framework for designing low-overhead distillation method that strategically leverages the cyclic symmetry of quantum BCH codes to determine which non-fault-tolerant circuits can successfully produce a fault-tolerant state. Numerical simulations on several high-performance quantum BCH codes up to 127 qubits demonstrate that our method achieves lower spatial overhead and logical error rates than conventional distillation circuits. Furthermore, we evaluated the logical error rates under a circuit-level noise model, and obtained performance benchmarks in realistic settings. This efficient state preparation technique is expected to contribute to the early realization of practical FTQCs, particularly on highly connected quantum platforms such as neutral atom systems.
one_liner: Cyclic symmetry enables lower-overhead fault-tolerant ancilla preparation for high-rate quantum BCH codes.
why_it_matters: Ancilla preparation is a major cost driver in fault-tolerant computing, and reducing it helps make high-rate codes more practical.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a two-stage ancilla-preparation method for quantum BCH codes: non-fault-tolerant preparation followed by tailored entanglement distillation. Its key design principle uses the cyclic symmetry of BCH codes to select circuits that can distill fault-tolerant states efficiently. Numerical simulations up to 127-qubit codes show reduced spatial overhead and lower logical error rates versus conventional distillation under circuit-level noise.
main_result: The proposed symmetry-aware distillation circuits prepare fault-tolerant ancillas for high-rate BCH codes with improved overhead and error benchmarks.
application_angle: The technique is relevant for early fault-tolerant machines, especially highly connected platforms such as neutral-atom processors.

title: Universal logic gates for coupled period-doubling systems
abstract: We propose a general architecture for universal logic operations using NAND and NOR gates on classical information encoded in period-doubled states of periodically-driven systems. The protocol involves applying a single pulse that simultaneously couple two input nodes with an output node. We show that the states of the nodes can be precisely controlled by tuning the coupling strength and pulse duration, allowing for robust logic gate operation. To highlight the universality of the protocol, we demonstrate its applicability on different systems, such as classical networks of dissi- pative parametric oscillators (DPO), quantum networks of Kerr parametric oscillators (KPO), and the periodically-driven open Dicke lattice model (DLM) emulating discrete time crystals (DTCs). We identify the parameter regimes in which the logic gate architecture is valid, and we showcase its robustness in the presence of fluctuations.
one_liner: Period-doubled driven systems can implement robust NAND and NOR logic through tuned multi-node coupling pulses.
why_it_matters: The proposal links time-crystalline or parametric-oscillator dynamics to universal logic operations that may inform unconventional computing architectures.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes universal classical logic gates encoded in period-doubled states of periodically driven systems. A single pulse couples two input nodes to an output node, with gate behavior controlled by coupling strength and pulse duration. The authors analyze dissipative parametric oscillators, Kerr parametric oscillator networks, and open Dicke lattice models, identifying regimes where NAND and NOR operation remains robust to fluctuations.
main_result: The work shows that tuned coupling pulses can realize universal NAND and NOR gates across several period-doubled oscillator platforms.
application_angle: Such robust logic primitives could support unconventional hardware based on driven nonlinear oscillators and time-crystalline phases.

title: Quantum-enhanced distributed network sensing using multiple quantum resources
abstract: We propose a theoretical scheme for quantum enhanced distributed network sensing, targeting multiphase estimation by leveraging multiple quantum resources. Specifically, we investigate the performance advantage in a distributed quantum network (DQN) for multiphase sensing by integrating three types of quantum resources(TQRs): quantum catalysis, entanglement, and squeezing. Our results reveal that employing all three TQRs leads to better sensing performance than using only two TQRs under both lossless and lossy conditions, with precision approaching the Heisenberg limit. We further demonstrate that partial quantum catalysis providesa stronger precision advantage than global catalysis in both ideal and noisy regimes. We identify a practical homodyne measurement scheme for globally and partially catalyzed multimode W type coherent states, whose measurement sensitivity can approach the corresponding quantum Cramer Rao bound. In this practical setting, partial catalysis also yields better measurement sensitivity than global catalysis. Moreover, under photon loss, both global and partial catalysis of multimode W type coherent states exhibit a loss catalysis dual enhanced sensitivity region. These findings highlight the quantum-enhanced advantages conferred by hybrid quantum resources for practical DQN sensing applications. Our work opens a way for realizing quantum-enhanced DQN sensing.
one_liner: Hybrid catalysis, entanglement, and squeezing improve distributed multiphase quantum sensing near Heisenberg-limited precision.
why_it_matters: Combining multiple optical quantum resources can increase networked sensing precision under realistic loss and measurement constraints.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes distributed multiphase sensing with three quantum resources: catalysis, entanglement, and squeezing. It finds that using all three resources outperforms two-resource configurations in both lossless and lossy regimes, with precision approaching the Heisenberg limit. For multimode W-type coherent states, a homodyne scheme can approach the quantum Cramer-Rao bound, and partial catalysis outperforms global catalysis in practical settings.
main_result: The scheme demonstrates enhanced multiphase sensitivity using catalyzed, entangled, squeezed multimode states with practical homodyne readout.
application_angle: It points toward deployable quantum-enhanced sensor networks for distributed phase estimation under optical loss.

title: Energy-efficient programmable integrated photonics via optimized Euler rotations
abstract: Programmable integrated photonics (PIP) has emerged as a powerful on-chip platform for optical signal processing and computing, enabling the implementation of reconfigurable N$\times$N unitary matrix transformations through meshes of tunable interferometers, which realize 2$\times$2 unitary matrices. However, the energy consumption associated with phase-shifter actuation is becoming a major limitation to the scalability of PIP platforms. Here, we introduce a geometric framework for energy optimization in PIP circuits by exploiting the representation of 2$\times$2 unitary matrices as concatenations of basic Euler rotations on the Bloch sphere. We show that equivalent implementations of the same N$\times$N unitary matrix (N $\geq$ 2) can exhibit markedly different energy costs depending on the rotation trajectories on the Bloch sphere implemented by each interferometer. Leveraging this insight, we identify minimum-energy configurations by systematically selecting the shortest rotation trajectories. We experimentally and numerically validate the proposed approach in diverse silicon PIP architectures, including feedforward and multipurpose hexagonal meshes, neural network accelerators, and photonic quantum-gate implementations. These results establish a general route toward more energy-efficient large-scale PIP processors for classical and quantum signal processing and computing applications.
one_liner: Optimized Euler-rotation choices reduce phase-shifter energy in programmable silicon photonic meshes.
why_it_matters: Lower actuation energy is a direct scalability lever for large programmable photonic processors used in computing and quantum optics.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce a geometric energy-optimization framework for programmable integrated photonics by mapping 2x2 interferometer operations to Euler rotations on the Bloch sphere. Equivalent decompositions of the same NxN unitary can require different phase-shifter trajectories, yielding different actuation costs. The method selects shorter rotation paths and is validated numerically and experimentally in silicon feedforward meshes, hexagonal meshes, neural accelerators, and photonic quantum-gate settings.
main_result: The work demonstrates that optimized Euler-rotation decompositions can reduce energy consumption for reconfigurable photonic unitary processors.
application_angle: The approach can improve the power scaling of integrated photonic computing, signal processing, and photonic quantum hardware.

title: Photolithography-Only Fabrication of Transmons Using Double-Oblique Evaporation
abstract: We investigate a photolithography-only fabrication process for transmon Josephson junctions using a modified double-oblique evaporation geometry. Using a bilayer resist process and Al shadow evaporation, we fabricate junction structures and confirm by optical and scanning electron microscopy that the resulting narrowed crossing region reaches a geometrical area on the order of $10^4~\mathrm{nm}^2$, which lies in the size range relevant to qubit junction fabrication. Room-temperature resistance screening shows that the junction resistance falls within the target range for the present transmon design over a usable process window and exhibits a clear design dependence. We further implement fabricated junctions in transmon devices and evaluate them in a three-dimensional Al cavity at $20 \, \mathrm{mK}$, where we observe basic transmon qubit operation with $f_{01}$=4.865 GHz, $T_1 \sim 9 \, μ\mathrm{s}$, and $T_2^* \sim 0.4 \, μ\mathrm{s}$. These results demonstrate the feasibility of realizing functional transmon devices in a photolithography-only process using double-oblique evaporation.
one_liner: Photolithography-only double-oblique evaporation yields functional Al transmon junctions without e-beam lithography.
why_it_matters: Replacing e-beam junction patterning with photolithography could simplify superconducting-qubit fabrication and improve process accessibility.
detailed_summary: The work develops a bilayer-resist, photolithography-only process for Al Josephson junctions using modified double-oblique evaporation. Microscopy confirms narrowed junction crossings with areas around 10^4 nm^2, and room-temperature resistance screening shows a usable design-dependent process window. Devices integrated into 3D Al cavities operate as transmons at 20 mK, with f01 = 4.865 GHz, T1 around 9 us, and T2* around 0.4 us.
main_result: The authors demonstrate basic transmon operation from photolithography-only junctions fabricated by double-oblique evaporation.
application_angle: The process points toward lower-barrier and potentially more scalable fabrication of superconducting-qubit hardware.

title: Quantum communications in continuous variable systems
abstract: Nowadays, quantum communications provide a vast field of research in rapid expansion, with a huge potential impact on the future developments of quantum technologies. In particular, continuous variable systems, employing coherent-state encoding and quadrature measurements, represent a suitable platform, due to their compatibility with both the modulation and detection systems currently employed in standard fiber-optical communications. In this work, we address some relevant aspects of the field, and provide innovative results being also experimentally oriented. In particular, we focus on two relevant paradigms: quantum decision theory and continuous variable quantum key distribution (CVQKD). In the former case, we address the problem of coherent-state discrimination and design new hybrid receivers for binary phase-shift keying discrimination, obtaining a quantum advantage over conventional detection schemes, being also robust against typical experimental imperfections. In the latter scenario, we proceed in two different directions. On the one hand, we design new CVQKD protocols employing discrete modulation of coherent states, being a feasible solution compatible with the state of the art in optical communications technologies. On the other hand, we address the more fundamental problem of performing channel losses mitigation to enhance existing protocols, and investigate the role of optical amplifiers for the task. Finally, we make a first step towards a fully non-Gaussian CVQKD scheme by proposing, for the first time, the adoption of an optimized state-discrimination receiver, commonly adopted for quantum decision theory, within the context of CVQKD, obtaining a genuine quantum enhancement over conventional protocols.
one_liner: Continuous-variable optical communication schemes improve coherent-state discrimination and CVQKD with experimentally feasible receivers.
why_it_matters: The work targets quantum communication protocols compatible with standard fiber modulation and quadrature detection hardware.
detailed_summary: This paper studies continuous-variable quantum communication using coherent-state encoding and quadrature-compatible measurement approaches. It designs hybrid receivers for binary phase-shift-keyed coherent-state discrimination that outperform conventional detection and remain robust to experimental imperfections. It also proposes discrete-modulation CVQKD protocols, analyzes optical-amplifier-based loss mitigation, and introduces optimized state-discrimination receivers into non-Gaussian CVQKD.
main_result: The main result is a set of receiver and protocol designs giving quantum-enhanced discrimination and CVQKD performance under realistic optical-communication constraints.
application_angle: The schemes are relevant to deployable quantum-secure communication over existing fiber-optical technology stacks.

title: Coherent Molecular Deceleration via Vibrational Bichromatic Force
abstract: We propose a scheme for direct laser deceleration of molecules based on a vibrational transition-mediated bichromatic force (VBCF). By precisely engineering mid-infrared optical fields, we establish coherent absorption-stimulated emission cycles while exploiting the long lifetime of vibrational excited states to suppress spontaneous decay and decoherence, rendering the deceleration process effectively non-dissipative. Unlike schemes based on electronic transitions, our approach completely circumvents the restrictive Franck-Condon factors. Using the fundamental vibrational transition of $^{13}$CO$_2$ as a test case, we achieve a deceleration of $1.45\times 10^5$~m/s$^2$ with negligible population loss over the full interaction time. This VBCF framework provides a general route to cold molecules applicable to any species with an allowed fundamental vibrational transition, opening broad prospects in cold chemistry and quantum metrology.
one_liner: A mid-infrared vibrational bichromatic force can coherently decelerate molecules without relying on electronic cycling transitions.
why_it_matters: By bypassing Franck-Condon constraints, the approach could broaden direct laser slowing to many molecular species useful in metrology and cold chemistry.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes vibrational-transition-mediated bichromatic force deceleration using engineered mid-infrared fields. Coherent absorption and stimulated-emission cycles exploit long vibrational lifetimes to suppress spontaneous decay and make the slowing effectively non-dissipative. For the fundamental vibrational transition of 13CO2, simulations show 1.45e5 m/s^2 deceleration with negligible population loss over the interaction time.
main_result: The authors predict strong coherent molecular deceleration via a vibrational bichromatic force with negligible loss.
application_angle: A species-general route to cold molecules would support precision molecular spectroscopy, quantum metrology, and controlled chemistry.

title: Sensitivity Bounds of Multiparameter Metrology at Thermal Equilibrium
abstract: Quantum metrology aims to enhance measurement precision beyond the classical limit by leveraging quantum resources. Unlike multi-parameter dynamic quantum metrology, many questions regarding multiparameter quantum metrology at thermal equilibrium remain elusive. In particular, the ultimate precision limits achievable in this equilibrium setting are not yet well understood. In this work, we examine the fundamental limits of estimating multiple parameters with a quantum probe at thermal equilibrium. We first show that the Heisenberg limit with respect to the number of probes can be achieved, and our bound coincides with the known single-parameter bound when only one parameter is estimated. We then consider the low temperature limit, revealing a qualitatively different behavior compared to the finite temperature case. We give an example to illustrate the usage of our main results. Finally, we show the conditions under which the sensitivity bound can be attained and the optimal measurements to achieve it.
one_liner: Thermal-equilibrium multiparameter metrology can reach Heisenberg scaling under derived attainability and measurement conditions.
why_it_matters: The results clarify ultimate precision limits for equilibrium probes, informing sensor design where parameters are encoded in thermal states.
detailed_summary: This theoretical work derives sensitivity bounds for estimating multiple parameters with quantum probes at thermal equilibrium. It shows that Heisenberg scaling in the number of probes is achievable and recovers known single-parameter bounds as a special case. The analysis distinguishes finite- and low-temperature regimes, gives an illustrative example, and identifies attainability conditions and optimal measurements.
main_result: The paper establishes multiparameter sensitivity bounds at thermal equilibrium and conditions for saturating them.
application_angle: These bounds can guide practical quantum sensors operating near equilibrium, especially when multiple fields or couplings must be inferred simultaneously.

title: Terrestrial readiness campaign for space-to-ground quantum communications with a space-qualified entangled photon-pair system
abstract: Realizing a global quantum internet relies on the deployment of robust satellite-based entanglement distribution links. While pioneering demonstrations have established the feasibility of such links, the transition to operational infrastructure demands the validation of robust, integrated space-to-ground architectures. Here, we report on a free-space Quantum Key Distribution experiment conducted over a 1.8 km free-space link using an engineering model of the quantum payload onboard the SpeQtre satellite and the Abu Dhabi Quantum Optical Ground Station. By implementing a BBM92 protocol with polarization-entangled photons, a secret key rate of approximately 7.56 kbps with a mean quantum bit error rate of 4.78%+-0.24% was produced. The deployed system featured spectral and spatial filtering approaches identical to those in the space segment, thus validating the link budget and background rejection capabilities under realistic atmospheric conditions. These results confirm the operational compatibility between the ground and space segments, establishing a critical performance baseline for the SpeQtre mission and future space-based, large-scale quantum networks.
one_liner: A SpeQtre engineering payload delivered entanglement-based BBM92 QKD over a 1.8 km terrestrial free-space link.
why_it_matters: The campaign validates payload-ground-station compatibility and link-budget assumptions before operational space-to-ground entanglement distribution.
detailed_summary: The experiment used a space-qualified entangled photon-pair system matching the SpeQtre payload architecture with the Abu Dhabi Quantum Optical Ground Station. Over a 1.8 km free-space link, the system implemented polarization-entanglement BBM92 QKD and produced about 7.56 kbps secret key rate. The measured mean QBER was 4.78% +/- 0.24%, with spectral and spatial filtering matching the planned space segment to test background rejection and link performance.
main_result: The authors demonstrate terrestrial BBM92 QKD with a SpeQtre engineering payload at 7.56 kbps secret key rate and 4.78% mean QBER.
application_angle: This de-risks satellite QKD deployment by confirming realistic ground-space interface performance under atmospheric conditions.

title: High-fidelity molecular quantum logic gates resilient to interaction fluctuation
abstract: Optically trapped polar molecules are promising for quantum information processing, yet the accuracy of an entangling molecular gate is limited by the uncertainty of dipole-dipole interactions~(DDI) from the molecular motion in traps. We show that two $π$ pulses of global microwave excitation can yield a high-fidelity controlled-phase gate when assisted by two single-qubit gates. The gate is resilient to the uncertainty of DDI because it does not rely on populating DDI-coupled states. Further, the controlled phase is fully tunable by varying the relative phase of the two global microwave pulses, and, hence, the gate can find applications in a wide range of quantum algorithms involving quantum Fourier transform. Moreover, we introduce a motional-mode separation technique to quantum mechanically study the influence of the molecular motion, which shows that the gate fidelity can be over 0.9999 with typical experimental conditions.
one_liner: A microwave-pulse controlled-phase gate for trapped polar molecules is resilient to dipole-dipole interaction fluctuations.
why_it_matters: Interaction-insensitive entangling gates address a key fidelity bottleneck for molecular quantum processors using moving trapped molecules.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a molecular controlled-phase gate using two global microwave pi pulses assisted by two single-qubit gates. Because the protocol avoids populating dipole-dipole-coupled states, it is robust against interaction uncertainty caused by molecular motion in optical traps. The controlled phase is tunable through the relative microwave-pulse phase, and a motional-mode separation analysis predicts fidelity above 0.9999 under typical experimental conditions.
main_result: The authors propose a tunable controlled-phase molecular gate with predicted fidelity exceeding 0.9999 despite dipole-dipole fluctuation.
application_angle: Robust high-fidelity entangling gates strengthen the case for polar-molecule platforms in scalable quantum algorithms and simulation.

title: Capacity of multimode quantum Gaussian channels
abstract: We derive explicit formulas for the capacity of multimode quantum Gaussian channels which serve as a fundamental model for optical version of multiple-input multiple-output channels. We show that it is always optimal to increase the number of modes under fixed power constraint. We derive an analytical formula for the ensemble-averaged Holevo capacity in the case of random passive transformations. The analogous results are also obtained for capacities achievable under homodyne and heterodyne detection. We further discuss the generalization of the model to include weak active transformations.
one_liner: Derives explicit capacity formulas for multimode quantum Gaussian optical MIMO channels under power constraints.
why_it_matters: Gives communication engineers analytic limits and receiver benchmarks for high-dimensional optical quantum links.
detailed_summary: The paper derives capacity formulas for multimode quantum Gaussian channels, modeling optical multiple-input multiple-output communication. It proves that, at fixed total power, using more modes is always optimal and gives ensemble-averaged Holevo capacities for random passive transformations. It also treats capacities achievable with homodyne and heterodyne detection and discusses weak active transformations.
main_result: Explicit capacity and receiver-specific achievable-rate formulas are obtained for passive multimode Gaussian channel ensembles.
application_angle: The results provide design limits for spatial, frequency, or temporal multiplexing in quantum and classical optical communication systems.

title: Off-line quantum-advantage feature extraction for industrial production
abstract: Quantum computing is no longer a lab curiosity for academic research. Industrial processors exceeding 100 qubits are commercially accessible and, for the first time, can extract information from data in ways that classical algorithms struggle to match. The most direct way to monetize this capability for industrial production today is quantum feature extraction: turning raw business data (images, customer records, molecules, or sensor readings) into richer representations that outperform standard machine learning models. There is one obstacle, however, that stands between today's demonstrations and tomorrow's production systems: every sample of data costs a quantum computing execution. For a company with millions of customers, satellite images, or transactions per month, processing every sample on quantum hardware is simply not viable. This work introduces quantum feature surrogates, a framework developed by Kipu Quantum that breaks this bottleneck. The idea is intuitive though challenging: instead of asking the quantum computer to look at every single sample, we let it look at a small, carefully chosen subsample of the data, whose distribution faithfully represents the full set. A simple classical model, a surrogate, then learns the quantum-induced patterns and applies them to the rest of the dataset at near-zero cost. The quantum processor stops being a per-sample engine and becomes a teacher of representations, while production inference runs entirely on classical hardware.
one_liner: Introduces quantum feature surrogates so quantum processors teach representations offline while classical models handle production inference.
why_it_matters: Targets a practical bottleneck in industrial quantum ML: avoiding one quantum execution per data sample at deployment scale.
detailed_summary: The work proposes quantum feature surrogates, where a quantum processor evaluates a carefully selected data subsample to generate quantum-induced feature patterns. A classical surrogate model then learns those patterns and applies them across the full dataset without further quantum executions. This reframes current quantum hardware as an offline representation teacher rather than a per-sample inference engine.
platform_or_system: commercial quantum processors for feature extraction
main_result: A framework is presented for amortizing quantum feature generation over large industrial datasets using classical surrogate inference.
application_angle: It makes quantum-enhanced feature pipelines more compatible with high-volume production workloads in imaging, chemistry, finance, and sensing.

title: Coherent All-Optical Radio Frequency Phase Sensing Using Multiphoton Dressing and Interference
abstract: Multi-photon dressing and interference in atomic systems is a key to several cutting edge technologies like Rydberg atom radio frequency sensors, clocks and magnetometers because it enables the engineering of atomic properties. Rydberg atom sensors are attracting significant interest because they can be used for applications where it is difficult or impossible to use conventional antennas, opening a number of new opportunities in fields like communications, test and measurement and radar. To date, radio frequency field amplitude detection is well-established in Rydberg electrometry. Phase detection, which is crucial for encoding radio frequency signals, typically requires an external heterodyning field or an atomic closed-loop interferometer. The heterodyne method compromises the intrinsic transparency of the sensor to the radio frequency wave and its inherently broad carrier bandwidth, in addition to increasing its complexity by introducing a local oscillator. In prior theoretical work, aimed at overcoming the disadvantages of the heterodyne method, we theoretically investigated the possibility of using the oscillatory dynamics of an all-optical five-level closed loop to sense the phase and amplitude of the target radio frequency fields. In this work, we experimentally demonstrate the scheme. We determine the coherence time of the loop to be on the order of ms and show that in-phase and quadrature signals can be extracted from a radio frequency signal.
one_liner: Experimentally demonstrates all-optical RF phase sensing in Rydberg atoms using multiphoton dressing and closed-loop interference.
why_it_matters: RF phase readout without an external local oscillator preserves sensor transparency and simplifies broadband Rydberg electrometry.
detailed_summary: The paper experimentally realizes a five-level all-optical closed-loop Rydberg scheme for sensing radio-frequency phase and amplitude. Multiphoton dressing and interference encode the target RF phase into optical atomic response, removing the need for a separate RF heterodyne local oscillator. The loop coherence time is measured on the millisecond scale, and in-phase and quadrature components are extracted from the RF signal.
main_result: The experiment demonstrates coherent all-optical extraction of RF I/Q signals with millisecond-scale closed-loop coherence.
application_angle: This supports compact, transparent RF receivers for communications, test and measurement, and radar environments where conventional antennas are limiting.

title: Unveiling Energetic Advantage in Superconducting Cat-Qubits Quantum Computation
abstract: Quantum computers are emerging as a promising new technology due to their ability to solve complex problems that exceed the capabilities of classical systems in terms of time. Among various implementations, superconducting qubits have become the leading technology due to their scalability and compatibility with quantum error correction mechanisms. Although time has traditionally been the primary focus, energetic efficiency is becoming an increasingly important consideration, especially with the possibility of a quantum energetic advantage. In this article, the energy consumption of the Semiclassical Quantum Fourier Transform was analyzed on a superconducting quantum computing platform based on cat qubits. Quantum error correction mechanisms were studied and considered in the energy estimations. The results show how the energy consumption scales with the number of qubits and how the most relevant parameters required for qubit stabilization, gate implementation, and error correction codes contribute to the overall energy usage. An optimization method was developed to tune these parameters with the goal of minimizing energy consumption while maintaining qubit fidelities above a given threshold. Additionally, a comparative study with state-of-the-art classical computers indicates a potential quantum energetic advantage for systems with more than 26 qubits, assuming cryogenic systems operating at Carnot efficiency, with this energetic advantage arising before any computational advantage. This behavior persists even when realistic cryogenic systems and control electronics are taken into account.
one_liner: Analyzes energy consumption of semiclassical QFT on superconducting cat qubits with stabilization and error correction included.
why_it_matters: Moves quantum hardware benchmarking beyond speed by quantifying when a protected cat-qubit processor could use less energy than classical machines.
detailed_summary: The study estimates energy use for the semiclassical Quantum Fourier Transform on superconducting cat-qubit hardware, including stabilization, gate implementation, and error-correction contributions. It develops an optimization procedure that tunes physical parameters to minimize energy while enforcing fidelity thresholds. The comparison suggests a possible energetic advantage beyond 26 qubits under ideal Carnot cryogenics, with persistence under more realistic cooling and control assumptions.
main_result: Energy scaling and parameter optimization indicate a potential quantum energetic advantage for cat-qubit QFT before computational advantage appears.
application_angle: The analysis informs hardware design tradeoffs for scalable, energy-aware superconducting quantum processors.

title: Twisted light generates robust many-body states for practical quantum computing
abstract: Twisted light carries orbital angular momentum (OAM) and can drive excitations of confined, interacting electrons that are dark to uniform dipolar probes. Here we show how this ``beyond-Kohn's-Theorem'' optical channel can become a concrete control primitive for quantum computing. Correlation sectors in few-electron quantum dots -- characterized by the relative angular momentum quantum number -- form a tunable ladder of many-body states that are robust in the limited sense of symmetry-protected selection rules and persistent chiral spectroscopic fingerprints; full topological gap protection requires three or more electrons. A twisted-light pulse with prescribed OAM index and polarization provides fast optical write, read, and scalable addressing of these sectors via the selection rule $Δ|m|=\pm(l+σ)$. In the analytically solvable Calogero ($1/r^2$) interaction limit, both the energy spectrum and the twisted-light matrix elements are closed-form functions of the interaction strength, allowing gate parameters (Rabi frequency, qubit frequency, anharmonicity, and leakage rates) to be written down explicitly. We map these results onto a universal single-qubit gate set, propose a concrete two-qubit entangling mechanism via state-dependent Coulomb coupling between adjacent dots, and identify the dominant decoherence channel (quadrupolar charge noise). A semi-analytic $N=3$ extension using the $1/N$ expansion provides a design-level scaffold for the topological roadmap, including quasihole sector addressing. The central operational message is that twisted light enables WRITE (pulse-create a correlation sector), READ (spectroscopically diagnose correlations), and SCALE (optical addressing via spatial light modulator) in a unified photonic control layer. Throughout, screened and Coulomb interactions preserve the same qualitative chiral fingerprints established in the solvable limit.
one_liner: Proposes twisted-light control of correlation sectors in few-electron quantum dots as optically addressable qubit states.
why_it_matters: Connects orbital-angular-momentum photonics to concrete write, read, and scaling primitives for quantum-dot quantum computing.
detailed_summary: The paper shows how orbital-angular-momentum light can excite correlation sectors of confined interacting electrons that are inaccessible to uniform dipole probes. In a solvable Calogero interaction limit, spectra and twisted-light matrix elements are expressed analytically, enabling estimates of Rabi rates, qubit frequencies, anharmonicity, and leakage. It maps these sectors to single-qubit gates, proposes state-dependent Coulomb coupling between adjacent dots, and outlines an N=3 path toward topological protection.
platform_or_system: few-electron quantum dots driven by OAM light
main_result: Twisted-light pulses are proposed as a unified optical layer for writing, reading, and addressing many-body quantum-dot qubit states.
application_angle: The approach could enable spatially programmable photonic control of dense quantum-dot processors using structured light hardware.

title: Developing a photon-number-resolving detection chain for quantum communication protocols involving mesoscopic states of light
abstract: We present the characterization of a photon-number-resolving detection chain based on Silicon photomultipliers (SiPM) coupled to a 14 bit, 1 Gs\s digital acquisition system embedding an FPGA-based signal processing pipeline that performs real-time baseline subtraction, digital deconvolution, and charge integration. Three SiPM models manufactured by Hamamatsu are tested and compared in the mesoscopic intensity regime using both classical coherent states and quantum twin-beam states, enabling a systematic investigation of the effects of pixel pitch, pile-up, and photon detection efficiency on the detector performance.
one_liner: Characterizes a SiPM-based photon-number-resolving detection chain for mesoscopic quantum communication states.
why_it_matters: Photon-number resolution at mesoscopic intensities is a key detector capability for practical quantum communication protocols beyond single photons.
detailed_summary: The work presents a detection chain combining silicon photomultipliers with a 14-bit, 1 Gs/s acquisition system and FPGA real-time processing. The pipeline performs baseline subtraction, digital deconvolution, and charge integration to support photon-number-resolving measurements. Three Hamamatsu SiPM models are compared using coherent states and quantum twin-beam states to quantify effects from pixel pitch, pile-up, and photon detection efficiency.
main_result: A real-time FPGA-assisted SiPM readout chain is characterized for resolving mesoscopic optical photon numbers.
application_angle: The detector architecture is relevant for deployable quantum communication experiments using bright or mesoscopic nonclassical light.

title: Induced transitions in non-Hermitian spin-boson models with time-dependent boundaries
abstract: We study a time-dependent non-Hermitian extension of the Schütte-Da~Providência spin-boson Hamiltonian with complex couplings. A time-dependent Dyson map containing a squeezing transformation maps the model, in an admissible bounded regime, to a Hermitian Hamiltonian with real instantaneous energy spectrum. The squeezing contribution generates a dilatation term allowing the Hermitian partner to be interpreted as a fixed-domain representation of a system with moving boundaries. While the fixed-boundary Hermitian model conserves $Q=N-S_0$ and forbids transitions between sectors differing by two bosonic quanta, the boundary motion opens such channels. For closed boundary protocols with constant background parameters the first-order integrated transition amplitude vanishes, reflecting the unitary nature of constant squeezing. Nontrivial transition control arises when the non-Hermitian parameter varies during the boundary motion, changing the dressed basis and allowing boundary-induced transitions to be suppressed or enhanced by coherent interference.
one_liner: Time-varying non-Hermitian spin-boson boundaries can open and coherently control otherwise forbidden bosonic transition channels.
why_it_matters: The work gives a controlled route to engineer transitions in non-Hermitian photonic or oscillator-like systems via boundary motion and parameter modulation.
detailed_summary: The paper studies a non-Hermitian Schutte-Da Providencia spin-boson Hamiltonian with complex, time-dependent couplings. A bounded Dyson map with squeezing transforms the model to a Hermitian Hamiltonian with real instantaneous spectrum, where the squeezing term acts as a dilatation associated with moving boundaries. Boundary motion breaks the fixed-boundary selection rule that forbids transitions between sectors differing by two bosonic quanta, while variation of the non-Hermitian parameter enables coherent suppression or enhancement.
main_result: It shows that time-dependent boundaries can induce otherwise forbidden two-boson-sector transitions and that non-Hermitian parameter modulation controls their interference.
application_angle: The mechanism is relevant to tunable photonic, cavity, or oscillator platforms where boundary modulation can be used as a transition-control knob.

title: Quantum analysis of multi-frequency laser with photonic time crystal
abstract: The present study considers the operation of a laser that incorporates a photonic time crystal (PTC), the purpose of which is to generate a field characterised by multiple widely separated optical frequencies. This laser is the subject of both a proposal and theoretical investigation. The laser comprises an active medium and a PTC within a small cavity constructed from two photonic crystals that are positioned in an overlapping configuration. PTC is modulated by an external field. The spikes in the laser field spectrum are separated by the PTC modulation frequency.
